# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCEOT503]**
> **Define Battery Energy Storage System (BESS). List any two advantages of Li-ion batteries over lead-acid batteries for grid-scale applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** A BESS is an electrochemical storage device that stores electrical energy via reversible redox reactions between two electrodes and an electrolyte, releasing it as DC electricity on demand.

> **Advantages of Li-ion over Lead-Acid (2 marks):**
> 1. **Higher energy density** — Li-ion delivers 150–250 Wh/kg versus 30–50 Wh/kg for lead-acid, reducing footprint by 3–5×.
> 2. **Longer cycle life** — 3,000–10,000 cycles at 80 % DoD vs. 500–1,500 for lead-acid.
> 3. *(any one additional)*: Lower self-discharge (0.1 %/day vs. 3–5 %/day), higher round-trip efficiency (90–95 % vs. 70–80 %), no memory effect.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCEOT503]**
> **What is a supercapacitor? Why is it preferred over a battery for short-duration, high-power applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A supercapacitor (also called an EDLC — Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor) stores energy electrostatically at the interface between an activated-carbon electrode (surface area 1,500–3,000 m²/g) and an electrolyte, with a charge-separation distance of ~1 nm.

> **Why preferred for short-duration, high-power (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Power density** of 10,000–100,000 W/kg vs. ~1,000 W/kg for Li-ion — meaning 10–100× faster charge/discharge.
> 2. **Cycle life** of 0.5–1 million cycles vs. 3,000–10,000 for batteries — vastly superior for frequent cycling applications like frequency regulation and regenerative braking.
> 3. **Sub-second response time** (~1 ms) for ride-through and voltage sag mitigation.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> ### 🔷 QUESTION A — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCEOT503, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operating principle of a **grid-scale Battery Energy Storage System (BESS)**. Discuss the role of the **Battery Management System (BMS)** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5 MWh BESS operates with an average internal resistance of 0.05 Ω per cell, with 200 cells in series at 3.7 V each. It is cycled daily at the **0.5C rate**. Calculate:
   (i) the **pack voltage**,
   (ii) the **discharge current** at 0.5C,
   (iii) the **round-trip efficiency** (assume 1.0C and 0.5C cycles for charge and discharge respectively for loss calculation).
**(7 marks)**

---

### 📝 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — BESS Architecture & BMS (7 marks):**

A grid-scale BESS consists of:

1. **Battery cells (electrochemical core)** — Li-ion cells, the smallest storage unit.
2. **Modules** — series-parallel combination of cells to achieve desired voltage and capacity.
3. **Rack / Container** — multiple modules housed in a climate-controlled enclosure (typically 1–6 MWh per container).
4. **Power Conversion System (PCS)** — bidirectional inverter (AC→DC during charge, DC→AC during discharge) with IGBT/SiC switches.
5. **Battery Management System (BMS)** — the "brain" of the BESS.
6. **Transformer & Switchgear** — steps up voltage and connects to grid.
7. **SCADA / Energy Management System (EMS)** — supervises dispatch and grid-services.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functions of BMS (key syllabus highlight):**
> - **Monitoring** — voltage, current, temperature of every cell (via cell-monitoring ICs like LTC6811).
> - **Protection** — over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, over-temperature, short-circuit trips.
> - **Balancing** — passive (resistor bleeding) or active (DC-DC shuttling) to equalize cell SoC.
> - **State estimation** — SoC (Coulomb counting or Kalman filter), SoH (capacity fade), SoP (power capability).
> - **Communication** — CAN, Modbus, IEC 61850 to SCADA.
> - **Thermal management** — HVAC/LC control to keep cells at 20–35 °C.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Energy capacity $E = 5$ MWh
- Internal resistance per cell $R_{\text{cell}} = 0.05 \, \Omega$
- Number of series cells $N_s = 200$
- Nominal cell voltage $V_{\text{cell}} = 3.7$ V
- C-rate = 0.5C (discharge), 1.0C (charge, for loss calculation)

**Step 1 — Pack voltage:**

$$V_{\text{pack}} \;=\; N_s \times V_{\text{cell}} \;=\; 200 \times 3.7 \;=\; 740 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Total energy in Wh → Ah conversion:**

$$Q_{\text{bat}} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}}}{V_{\text{pack}}} \;=\; \dfrac{5 \times 10^6 \text{ Wh}}{740 \text{ V}} \;=\; 6{,}756.76 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 3 — Discharge current at 0.5C:**

$$I_{\text{dis}} \;=\; 0.5 \times Q_{\text{bat}} \;=\; 0.5 \times 6{,}756.76 \;=\; 3{,}378.38 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Charge current at 1.0C (assumed):**

$$I_{\text{chg}} \;=\; 1.0 \times 6{,}756.76 \;=\; 6{,}756.76 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent pack resistance:**

$$R_{\text{pack}} \;=\; N_s \times R_{\text{cell}} \;=\; 200 \times 0.05 \;=\; 10 \, \Omega$$

**Step 6 — Energy loss during charge:**

$$E_{\text{loss,chg}} \;=\; I_{\text{chg}}^2 \times R_{\text{pack}} \times t_{\text{chg}} \;=\; I_{\text{chg}}^2 \times R_{\text{pack}} \times \dfrac{Q_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{chg}}} \;=\; I_{\text{chg}} R_{\text{pack}} Q_{\text{bat}}$$

$$E_{\text{loss,chg}} \;=\; 6{,}756.76 \times 10 \times 6{,}756.76 \;=\; 4.567 \times 10^8 \text{ W·s} \;=\; 126.86 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 7 — Energy loss during discharge (at 0.5C):**

$$E_{\text{loss,dis}} \;=\; I_{\text{dis}} R_{\text{pack}} Q_{\text{bat}} \;=\; 3{,}378.38 \times 10 \times 6{,}756.76 \;=\; 2.283 \times 10^8 \text{ W·s} \;=\; 63.43 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 8 — Round-trip efficiency:**

$$E_{\text{loss,total}} \;=\; 126.86 + 63.43 \;=\; 190.29 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\eta_{\text{RT}} \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{E_{\text{loss,total}}}{E_{\text{bat}}}\right) \times 100 \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{190.29 \times 10^3}{5 \times 10^6}\right) \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{RT}} \;=\; 96.19 \,\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Ah ↔ Wh conversion**. Always check units — 1 Wh = 1 V × 1 Ah. Also, "C-rate" is a multiplier on the **rated Ah capacity**, not the rated Wh. Many lose 1–2 marks by mixing these up.

---

> ### 🔶 QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCEOT503, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the operating principle of a **supercapacitor (CESS)** with reference to the **Helmholtz double-layer** model. Derive the expression for energy stored in a capacitor. Compare the energy density and power density of a supercapacitor with a Li-ion battery. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **molten-salt Thermal Energy Storage System (Thermal SS)** uses a 60-40 NaNO₃-KNO₃ mixture (specific heat = 1.45 kJ/(kg·K)) to store 1,500 MWh of thermal energy. The salt is heated from 290 °C to 565 °C. Calculate:
   (i) the **mass of salt required**,
   (ii) the **peak electric power output** if the storage is discharged over **7.5 hours** (assume turbine-generator efficiency = 38 %).
**(7 marks)**

---

### 📝 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — CESS Principle & Comparison (7 marks):**

A supercapacitor stores energy at the **electrochemical double layer** (Helmholtz layer) — a region of ~1 nm thickness at the electrode/electrolyte interface where oppositely charged ions line up on either side, forming a nanoscale "capacitor."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why supercapacitors have huge capacitance:** Activated-carbon electrodes provide **1,500–3,000 m²/g** of surface area. The capacitance formula $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$, with $A$ enormous and $d$ ~1 nm, gives capacitances of 1,000–10,000 F per cell.

**Energy stored derivation (already shown in Section 3.1):**
$$E_C \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$$

**Comparison with Li-ion:**

| Property | Supercapacitor | Li-ion BESS |
|---|---|---|
| Energy density (Wh/kg) | 5–10 | 150–250 |
| Power density (W/kg) | 10,000–100,000 | 500–2,000 |
| Cycle life | 500,000–1,000,000 | 3,000–10,000 |

**Key inference:** Supercapacitors trade **~25× lower energy density** for **~50× higher power density** and **~100× longer cycle life**, making them ideal for **high-power, short-duration, frequent-cycling** applications.

---

**Part (b) — Thermal SS Sizing (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Thermal energy to store $Q = 1{,}500$ MWh
- Specific heat $c_p = 1{,}450$ J/(kg·K)
- Cold temperature $T_C = 290$ °C = 563 K
- Hot temperature $T_H = 565$ °C = 838 K
- $\Delta T = 275$ K
- Discharge time $T = 7.5$ h
- Turbine-generator efficiency $\eta_T = 0.38$

**Step 1 — Convert Q to Joules:**

$$Q \;=\; 1{,}500 \text{ MWh} \times 3{,}600 \text{ s/h} \times 10^6 \text{ W/MW} \;=\; 5.4 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of salt required (sensible heat storage formula):**

$$Q \;=\; m \, c_p \, \Delta T$$

$$m \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{c_p \, \Delta T} \;=\; \dfrac{5.4 \times 10^{12}}{1{,}450 \times 275}$$

$$\boxed{m \;=\; 13{,}540{,}000 \text{ kg} \;\approx\; 13{,}540 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Step 3 — Peak thermal power:**

$$P_{\text{thermal}} \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{T} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}500 \text{ MWh}}{7.5 \text{ h}} \;=\; 200 \text{ MW}_{\text{th}}$$

**Step 4 — Peak electric power output (after turbine-generator):**

$$P_{\text{elec}} \;=\; \eta_T \times P_{\text{thermal}} \;=\; 0.38 \times 200$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{elec}} \;=\; 76 \text{ MW}_{\text{e}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Students often forget to convert **°C to K** for $\Delta T$ (numerically same for a difference, but the safer convention is K).
> 2. Confusing **MW thermal** with **MW electric** — always multiply by turbine efficiency. Noor-III CSP in Morocco discharges this exact 1,500 MWh / 7.5 h / 76 MW configuration.
> 3. Forgetting to convert MWh to Joules (× 3.6 × 10⁹) — losing 2 marks instantly.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

### 🔋 BESS (Battery Energy Storage System)
- ☐ Definition: **electrochemical** storage via reversible redox reactions.
- ☐ Sub-types: **Lead-Acid, Li-ion, NaS, VRFB** (Vanadium Redox Flow Battery).
- ☐ Architecture chain: **Cell → Module → Pack → PCS → Transformer → Grid**.
- ☐ **BMS** does monitoring, protection, balancing, SoC/SoH estimation, communication.
- ☐ Key formulas: $E = V \times Q \times \eta$; $\eta_{\text{RT}} = 1 - \frac{2 I R_{\text{int}}}{V_{\text{nom}}}$.
- ☐ Typical values: **Li-ion** — 90–95 % η_RT, 150–250 Wh/kg, 5,000 cycles.

### ⚡ CESS (Capacitor / Supercapacitor Energy Storage System)
- ☐ Definition: **electrostatic** storage at the **Helmholtz double layer**.
- ☐ Capacitance: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ — **A huge, d ~1 nm**.
- ☐ Energy: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$.
- ☐ Sub-types: **EDLC, pseudocapacitor, hybrid supercapacitor**.
- ☐ Use case: **frequency regulation, ride-through, regenerative braking** (not bulk energy).
- ☐ Power density: **10,000–100,000 W/kg**; cycle life **> 500,000**.

### 🔥 Thermal SS (Thermal Energy Storage System)
- ☐ Three modes: **Sensible (m c_p ΔT)**, **Latent (m L for PCM)**, **Thermochemical (n ΔH_r)**.
- ☐ Application: **CSP plants (Noor-III, Andasol, Solana)**.
- ☐ Common storage media: **molten salt, rocks/bed thermocline, concrete, PCM, ice**.
- ☐ Conversion chain: **Solar → Heat → Salt → Steam → Turbine → Grid**.
- ☐ Round-trip efficiency: **40–60 %** (limited by Carnot + turbine).

### 🎓 Universal ESS Comparison Pointers
- ☐ Highest **energy density**: **Li-ion BESS** (among these three).
- ☐ Highest **power density**: **CESS (supercapacitor)**.
- ☐ Longest **cycle life**: **CESS** (> 500,000).
- ☐ Best for **bulk grid storage**: **BESS** (Li-ion) or **Thermal SS** (with CSP).
- ☐ Best for **sub-cycle response**: **CESS**.
- ☐ Best for **renewable time-shift**: **BESS** + **Pumped Hydro**.
- ☐ Capital cost per kWh (2024): **Thermal SS < BESS < CESS**.

### 🧠 Must-Memorize Numerical Knobs
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- 1 Wh = 3,600 J; 1 MWh = $3.6 \times 10^9$ J
- Molten salt $c_p$ = **1.45 kJ/(kg·K)**; $\Delta T$ = **275 K** (290 °C → 565 °C)
- C-rate: $T_{\text{discharge}} = C_{\text{bat}} / I$ hours
- DoD + SoC = 1 (always)
- $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_C / T_H$ (use Kelvin!)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Curve - Load Duration Curve, Load Factor, Diversity Factor, Demand Factor, Plant Capacity Factor, Plant Use Factor (Numerical Problems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT503 - POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Load Curve - Load Duration Curve, Load Factor, Diversity Factor, Demand Factor, Plant Capacity Factor, Plant Use Factor (Numerical Problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Load Curve

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Load Curve** is a graphical plot showing the variation of electrical load (in kW or MW) on a power station as a function of **chronological time** (typically over 24 hours for a daily load curve, or over a year for an annual load curve). The area under the load curve directly represents the **total energy consumed** during that period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The *daily load curve* is the most frequently asked diagram. Always remember — *X-axis = Clock Time (h)*, *Y-axis = Load (kW/MW)*.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **heart-rate monitor graph** of a person for 24 hours. The heart beats faster (load rises) when the person runs or climbs stairs (morning office hours, evening AC usage), and beats slower (load falls) when the person rests (midnight, early dawn). A power station experiences a similar "heartbeat" — its load rises and falls with human activity. The **Load Curve** is exactly this "heart-rate graph" of the power station.

---

## 1.2 Load Duration Curve (LDC)

### Formal Definition
A **Load Duration Curve (LDC)** is obtained by rearranging the load curve in **descending order of magnitude** — that is, the highest loads are placed first, followed by progressively smaller loads, regardless of the chronological order in which they occurred. The X-axis represents the **number of hours** the load was sustained (duration), not the time of day.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Load Curve → time-of-day matters. Load Duration Curve → only the *magnitude* and *duration* matter; chronology is discarded.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **mountain range** sorted by height. The Load Curve is the mountain range as it stands geographically (left-to-right in time). The Load Duration Curve is the same mountain range re-arranged so the **tallest peak comes first, then the second tallest, and so on**, until the smallest hill. This re-arranged view helps engineers decide "for how many hours must the plant run at high capacity?"

---

## 1.3 Load Factor (LF)

### Formal Definition
**Load Factor** is the ratio of the **average load** over a given period to the **peak (maximum) load** occurring during that same period.

$$\text{Load Factor} = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Maximum Demand}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Always expressed as a **decimal** (0 to 1) or **percentage** (0% to 100%). A *higher* LF means the plant is being used more efficiently across time.

### Intuitive Analogy
A Load Factor is like a **school attendance ratio**. If a school has 1000 seats (peak demand) but on average only 700 are filled (average load), the LF = 0.7. A higher LF means a more uniformly utilized facility.

---

## 1.4 Diversity Factor (DF)

### Formal Definition
**Diversity Factor** is the ratio of the **sum of individual maximum demands** of various consumers (or substations/feeders) to the **maximum demand** on the power station as a whole.

$$\text{Diversity Factor} = \frac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Maximum Demand on Power Station}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Diversity Factor is **always greater than or equal to 1** ($\text{DF} \geq 1$). It is *never* less than 1, because consumers do not all peak at the exact same instant.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine **five different offices** in a building, each with their own peak AC usage. Office A peaks at 2 PM, Office B at 3 PM, etc. If we simply added all five peaks, we'd overestimate the building's total demand. The **Diversity Factor** captures this "spreading-out" effect — it tells the power station "you don't need to install capacity equal to the sum of every consumer's peak."

---

## 1.5 Demand Factor

### Formal Definition
**Demand Factor** is the ratio of the **maximum demand** of a consumer (or system) to the **connected load** (i.e., the sum of the rated capacities of all electrical apparatus connected to the supply).

$$\text{Demand Factor} = \frac{\text{Maximum Demand}}{\text{Connected Load}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Always $\leq 1$ because consumers never switch on **all** connected loads simultaneously.

### Intuitive Analogy
A household may have a connected load of 10 kW (all bulbs, fans, AC, geyser, washing machine combined). But the family never runs *everything* at the same time — at peak, they might use only 4 kW. So **Demand Factor = 4/10 = 0.4**.

---

## 1.6 Plant Capacity Factor (PCF)

### Formal Definition
**Plant Capacity Factor** is the ratio of the **actual energy generated** by the plant in a given period to the **maximum possible energy** that could have been generated if the plant had operated continuously at its **rated (installed) capacity** for the entire period.

$$\text{Plant Capacity Factor} = \frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times \text{Time Period}}$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **car's fuel efficiency benchmark**. The car has a 50-litre tank (plant capacity). Over a 24-hour trip, the engine (plant) generated only 35 litres worth of work, even though it could have generated 50. The PCF = 35/50 = 0.7.

---

## 1.7 Plant Use Factor (PUF)

### Formal Definition
**Plant Use Factor** is the ratio of energy actually generated to the energy that *could* have been generated had the plant been operating at its **rated capacity during the hours it was actually in use** (i.e., the plant's operational hours, not the total period).

$$\text{Plant Use Factor} = \frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times \text{Hours of Actual Use}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PUF vs PCF:** PUF uses *only the operational hours* in the denominator, so PUF is always $\geq$ PCF for the same energy output.

### Intuitive Analogy
A factory machine is rated to run at 1000 units/hour, but is **operational only for 6 hours a day** (not 24 hours). In those 6 hours, it produced 4500 units. The *Plant Use Factor* (during operating hours) = 4500 / (1000×6) = 0.75, while the *Plant Capacity Factor* (over the full day) = 4500 / (1000×24) = 0.1875.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are These Factors Important? — The "Why" Behind Each Metric

| # | Factor | What it Tells the Engineer | Real-World Use |
|---|--------|----------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Load Factor** | How *flat* is the load curve? | Determines tariff structures (HT/LT consumers). Higher LF = better equipment utilization. |
| 2 | **Diversity Factor** | How much do consumer peaks *overlap*? | Helps size the **installed capacity** of a power station. |
| 3 | **Demand Factor** | How much of the connected load is *actually* used at peak? | Used in sizing **transformers, switchgear, conductors** for a consumer. |
| 4 | **Plant Capacity Factor** | How heavily is the **plant's installed capacity** used over time? | Used to compare **economics** of different power plants (thermal, hydro, nuclear). |
| 5 | **Plant Use Factor** | How heavily is the plant used **when it actually runs**? | Used to compare **maintenance & downtime** impact. |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units Used in KTU Board Exams:**
> - Load → kW or MW
> - Energy → kWh or MWh
> - Time → hours (h)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Range | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|------|
| 1 | **Average Load** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Energy (E)}}{\text{Time Period (T)}}$ | $\leq$ Max Demand | kW |
| 2 | **Load Factor (LF)** | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Load}}{\text{Max. Demand}}$ | $0 \leq \text{LF} \leq 1$ | p.u. or % |
| 3 | **Diversity Factor (DF)** | $\dfrac{\sum \text{Individual Max. Demands}}{\text{Station Max. Demand}}$ | $\text{DF} \geq 1$ | pure number |
| 4 | **Demand Factor** | $\dfrac{\text{Max. Demand}}{\text{Connected Load}}$ | $0 \leq \text{DFac} \leq 1$ | p.u. or % |
| 5 | **Plant Capacity Factor (PCF)** | $\dfrac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times T}$ | $0 \leq \text{PCF} \leq 1$ | p.u. or % |
| 6 | **Plant Use Factor (PUF)** | $\dfrac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times t_{use}}$ | $0 \leq \text{PUF} \leq 1$ | p.u. or % |
| 7 | **Reserve Capacity** | $\text{Plant Capacity} - \text{Max. Demand}$ | $\geq 0$ | kW |
| 8 | **Utilization Factor** | $\dfrac{\text{Max. Demand}}{\text{Plant Capacity}}$ | $0 \leq \text{UF} \leq 1$ | p.u. or % |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When a problem gives you a *load schedule* (a table of time-vs-load), the **first** computation must always be the **Total Energy** (area under the curve) — *every other factor depends on it*.

---

## 2.3 Logical Steps to Solve a Typical KTU Numericals

The KTU board loves a **"Given Load Schedule → Find All Factors"** type problem. The systematic approach is:

1. **Plot/Read the Load Schedule** (time intervals with corresponding load values).
2. **Compute Total Energy** → $E = \sum (\text{Load}_i \times \text{Duration}_i)$ in kWh.
3. **Identify Maximum Demand** → the highest load value in the schedule.
4. **Compute Average Load** → $L_{avg} = \dfrac{E}{T}$ where $T$ = 24 h (for daily LDC).
5. **Compute Load Factor** → $LF = \dfrac{L_{avg}}{MD}$.
6. **Construct Load Duration Curve** → Re-arrange loads in descending order of magnitude.
7. **Compute Diversity, Demand, PCF, PUF** using their respective formulas (from additional given data).
8. **Convert to %** if required (most KTU answers accept both decimal and %).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tariff Design:** Electricity boards in India (KSEB, TNEB, etc.) use **Load Factor** to levy penalties — *lower LF = higher per-unit cost* (to penalize erratic consumers).
- **Capacity Planning:** **Diversity Factor** lets a power station install 1 transformer instead of 3, since not all feeders peak together.
- **Equipment Sizing:** **Demand Factor** decides the rating of a household main switch, MCB, and incoming cable.
- **Plant Investment Decisions:** **PCF** is the *primary* metric used by CEA (Central Electricity Authority, India) to compare thermal, hydro, and nuclear plants' productivity.
- **Maintenance Scheduling:** **PUF** helps in determining how much downtime a plant can afford for annual overhaul.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Problems & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Numerical Problem #1 (Full KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern - Model Problem A]**

### Problem Statement
A power station has the following **daily load cycle**:

| Time Interval (h) | Load (kW) |
|-------------------|-----------|
| 0 – 6 | 4000 |
| 6 – 10 | 12000 |
| 10 – 12 | 8000 |
| 12 – 16 | 16000 |
| 16 – 20 | 12000 |
| 20 – 22 | 6000 |
| 22 – 24 | 4000 |

**Additional Data:**
- Sum of individual maximum demands of consumers = 24000 kW
- Connected load = 20000 kW
- Installed (plant) capacity = 20000 kW
- Plant was actually in use for **20 hours** during the day

**Find:**
1. Plot the **Load Curve** and **Load Duration Curve** (qualitative).
2. **Total energy generated per day.**
3. **Average load** and **Load Factor**.
4. **Diversity Factor** and **Demand Factor**.
5. **Plant Capacity Factor** and **Plant Use Factor**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (a) — Total Energy Generated per Day

The total energy is the **area under the load curve**, computed as the sum of (Load × Time Duration) for each block.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= (4000 \times 6) + (12000 \times 4) + (8000 \times 2) + (16000 \times 4) \\
  &\quad + (12000 \times 4) + (6000 \times 2) + (4000 \times 2) \quad \text{[kWh]}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 24000 + 48000 + 16000 + 64000 + 48000 + 12000 + 8000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
E = 220000 \; \text{kWh/day}
$$

#### Part (b) — Average Load

$$
L_{avg} = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{220000}{24}
$$

$$
L_{avg} = 9166.67 \; \text{kW}
$$

#### Part (c) — Load Factor

From the table, the **Maximum Demand (MD)** is the highest load in the cycle:
- $MD = 16000$ kW (during 12 – 16 h).

$$
LF = \frac{L_{avg}}{MD} = \frac{9166.67}{16000}
$$

$$
LF = 0.5729 = 57.29\%
$$

#### Part (d) — Diversity Factor

$$
DF = \frac{\sum \text{Individual Max. Demands}}{\text{Station Max. Demand}} = \frac{24000}{16000}
$$

$$
DF = 1.5
$$

#### Part (e) — Demand Factor

$$
\text{Demand Factor} = \frac{\text{Max. Demand}}{\text{Connected Load}} = \frac{16000}{20000}
$$

$$
\text{Demand Factor} = 0.8 = 80\%
$$

#### Part (f) — Plant Capacity Factor (PCF)

$$
PCF = \frac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times T} = \frac{220000}{20000 \times 24}
$$

$$
PCF = \frac{220000}{480000}
$$

$$
PCF = 0.4583 = 45.83\%
$$

#### Part (g) — Plant Use Factor (PUF)

$$
PUF = \frac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times t_{use}} = \frac{220000}{20000 \times 20}
$$

$$
PUF = \frac{220000}{400000}
$$

$$
PUF = 0.55 = 55\%
$$

#### Part (h) — Qualitative Load Duration Curve

The loads sorted in **descending order** are:
- 16000 kW for 4 h
- 12000 kW for 8 h (since 12000 appears twice for 4 h each → 4 + 4 = 8 h)
- 8000 kW for 2 h
- 6000 kW for 2 h
- 4000 kW for 8 h (since 4000 appears twice for 6 h and 2 h → 6 + 2 = 8 h)

**Total:** 4 + 8 + 2 + 2 + 8 = 24 h ✓ (energy conservation check).

| Duration (h) | Load (kW) |
|--------------|-----------|
| 0 – 4 | 16000 |
| 4 – 12 | 12000 |
| 12 – 14 | 8000 |
| 14 – 16 | 6000 |
| 16 – 24 | 4000 |

---

## 3.2 Master Numerical Problem #2 (Alternative Question)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern - Model Problem B]**

### Problem Statement
A generating station has a **maximum demand** of 75 MW, a **load factor** of 0.6, a **diversity factor** of 1.5, a **demand factor** of 0.85, and an installed **plant capacity factor** of 50%. The plant was actually in use for 18 hours per day. Find:

1. **Average demand** of the station.
2. **Total energy generated per day**.
3. **Sum of individual maximum demands** of the consumers.
4. **Connected load** of the consumers.
5. **Installed (plant) capacity**.
6. **Plant use factor**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (1) — Average Demand

$$
L_{avg} = LF \times MD = 0.6 \times 75 \; \text{MW}
$$

$$
L_{avg} = 45 \; \text{MW}
$$

#### Part (2) — Total Energy Generated per Day

$$
E = L_{avg} \times T = 45 \; \text{MW} \times 24 \; \text{h}
$$

$$
E = 1080 \; \text{MWh/day}
$$

#### Part (3) — Sum of Individual Maximum Demands

$$
DF = \frac{\sum MD_i}{MD_{station}} \;\Rightarrow\; \sum MD_i = DF \times MD_{station}
$$

$$
\sum MD_i = 1.5 \times 75 \; \text{MW}
$$

$$
\sum MD_i = 112.5 \; \text{MW}
$$

#### Part (4) — Connected Load

$$
\text{Demand Factor} = \frac{MD}{\text{Connected Load}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Connected Load} = \frac{MD}{\text{DFac}}
$$

$$
\text{Connected Load} = \frac{75}{0.85}
$$

$$
\text{Connected Load} = 88.24 \; \text{MW}
$$

#### Part (5) — Installed Plant Capacity

$$
PCF = \frac{E}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times T} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Plant Capacity} = \frac{E}{PCF \times T}
$$

$$
\text{Plant Capacity} = \frac{1080}{0.5 \times 24} = \frac{1080}{12}
$$

$$
\text{Plant Capacity} = 90 \; \text{MW}
$$

#### Part (6) — Plant Use Factor

$$
PUF = \frac{E}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times t_{use}} = \frac{1080}{90 \times 18}
$$

$$
PUF = \frac{1080}{1620}
$$

$$
PUF = 0.6667 = 66.67\%
$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem #3 (Quick Practice)

> **[KTU Model Question - Sessional Test Pattern]**

### Problem
A power station supplies the following loads:
- 1500 kW between 6 AM – 10 AM
- 500 kW between 10 AM – 6 PM
- 2000 kW between 6 PM – 10 PM
- 1000 kW between 10 PM – 6 AM

Compute: (a) Energy supplied per day, (b) Average load, (c) Load factor.

### Solution

#### (a) Energy per Day

$$
E = (1500 \times 4) + (500 \times 8) + (2000 \times 4) + (1000 \times 8)
$$

$$
E = 6000 + 4000 + 8000 + 8000 = 26000 \; \text{kWh}
$$

#### (b) Average Load

$$
L_{avg} = \frac{26000}{24} = 1083.33 \; \text{kW}
$$

#### (c) Load Factor

The maximum demand is **2000 kW** (between 6 PM – 10 PM).

$$
LF = \frac{1083.33}{2000} = 0.5417 = 54.17\%
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart — How All Factors Relate to Load Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Daily Load Schedule] --> B[Compute Total Energy: E = sum Load x Time]
    A --> C[Identify Maximum Demand: MD]
    B --> D[Average Load: L_avg = E / T]
    D --> E[Load Factor: LF = L_avg / MD]
    C --> F[Diversity Factor: DF = sum MD_i / MD]
    C --> G[Demand Factor: DFac = MD / Connected Load]
    B --> H[Plant Capacity Factor: PCF = E / Cap x T]
    B --> I[Plant Use Factor: PUF = E / Cap x t_use]
    A --> J[Rearrange Loads in Descending Order]
    J --> K[Load Duration Curve LDC]
    A --> L[Plot Load vs Clock Time]
    L --> M[Load Curve]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Load Curve vs Load Duration Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC1[Step 1: Source Data]
        X1[Chronological Load Data: Load vs Time of Day]
    end
    subgraph SC2[Step 2A: Plot Chronologically]
        Y1[Load Curve: X axis is clock time]
    end
    subgraph SC3[Step 2B: Re sort Descending]
        Z1[Load Duration Curve: X axis is hours of duration]
    end
    subgraph SC4[Step 3: Engineering Insight]
        W1[For Tariff and Energy Audit: Use Load Curve]
        W2[For Capacity Sizing: Use Load Duration Curve]
    end
    X1 --> Y1
    X1 --> Z1
    Y1 --> W1
    Z1 --> W2
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy Diagram — Range and Constraint of Each Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[All Load Factors: 0 to Infinity Domain] --> R1[Load Factor: 0 to 1]
    R0 --> R2[Diversity Factor: 1 to Greater than 1]
    R0 --> R3[Demand Factor: 0 to 1]
    R0 --> R4[Plant Capacity Factor: 0 to 1]
    R0 --> R5[Plant Use Factor: 0 to 1]
    R1 --> S1[LF = 1 means flat constant load]
    R1 --> S2[LF less than 1 means peaky load]
    R2 --> T1[DF = 1 means all consumers peak together]
    R2 --> T2[DF greater than 1 means diversified peaks]
    R3 --> U1[DFac = 1 means all connected load used at once]
    R3 --> U2[DFac less than 1 means part load used]
    R4 --> V1[PCF = 1 means plant running full capacity all time]
    R5 --> W1[PUF = 1 means plant at full capacity during use hours]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Solving a KTU Numerical (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Read Given Load Schedule Table] --> P2{Identify Maximum Demand MD}
    P2 --> P3[Compute Total Energy E by sum of Load x Time]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Average Load L_avg = E by T]
    P4 --> P5[Load Factor LF = L_avg by MD]
    P2 --> P6{Additional Data Given}
    P6 -- Yes Sum of MD --> Q1[Diversity Factor DF = Sum MD by MD]
    P6 -- Yes Connected Load --> Q2[Demand Factor = MD by Connected Load]
    P6 -- Yes Plant Capacity --> Q3[Plant Capacity Factor PCF = E by Cap x T]
    P6 -- Yes Use Hours t --> Q4[Plant Use Factor PUF = E by Cap x t]
    P5 --> P7[Final Tabulated Result with Units]
    Q1 --> P7
    Q2 --> P7
    Q3 --> P7
    Q4 --> P7
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with their formulas:**
(a) Load Factor, (b) Diversity Factor, (c) Demand Factor.

#### Model Answer
(a) **Load Factor** is the ratio of the average load over a given period to the maximum demand occurring in that period:
$$\text{Load Factor} = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Maximum Demand}}$$

(b) **Diversity Factor** is the ratio of the sum of the individual maximum demands of consumers to the maximum demand on the power station:
$$\text{Diversity Factor} = \frac{\sum \text{Individual Max. Demands}}{\text{Station Max. Demand}}$$

(c) **Demand Factor** is the ratio of the maximum demand of a consumer to the connected load:
$$\text{Demand Factor} = \frac{\text{Maximum Demand}}{\text{Connected Load}}$$

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Load Curve and Load Duration Curve.** Why is the Load Duration Curve important in power station planning?

#### Model Answer

| Aspect | Load Curve | Load Duration Curve |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| X-axis | Clock time (h) | Duration of load (h) |
| Y-axis | Load (kW) | Load (kW) |
| Order | Chronological | Descending magnitude |
| Use | Tariff, daily planning | Capacity sizing, unit commitment |

**Importance of LDC:** It directly shows the number of hours a particular load level is sustained. This helps engineers decide:
- **Base load capacity** (load that runs 24 h).
- **Peak load capacity** (load that runs only a few hours).
- **Spinning reserve** requirements.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 1 Internal Choice

### Question B1. **[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Internal Choice – Pattern A]** (14 Marks)

A power station has the following daily load schedule:

| Time (h) | 0–6 | 6–9 | 9–12 | 12–16 | 16–20 | 20–22 | 22–24 |
|----------|-----|-----|------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Load (MW) | 20 | 40 | 50 | 75 | 60 | 30 | 15 |

Additional data:
- Sum of individual maximum demands = 120 MW
- Connected load = 100 MW
- Plant capacity = 90 MW
- Plant was in actual use for 20 hours.

**Find:**
(a) (7 Marks) Energy generated per day, average load, and load factor. Also draw the load duration curve.
(b) (7 Marks) Diversity factor, demand factor, plant capacity factor, and plant use factor.

---

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Total Energy E**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= (20 \times 6) + (40 \times 3) + (50 \times 3) + (75 \times 4) \\
  &\quad + (60 \times 4) + (30 \times 2) + (15 \times 2) \;[\text{MWh}]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
E = 120 + 120 + 150 + 300 + 240 + 60 + 30
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 1020 \; \text{MWh}}
$$

**Step 2: Average Load**

$$
L_{avg} = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{1020}{24} = 42.5 \; \text{MW}
$$

**Step 3: Load Factor**

Maximum Demand $MD = 75$ MW (during 12 – 16 h).

$$
LF = \frac{42.5}{75} = 0.5667 = 56.67\%
$$

**Step 4: Load Duration Curve (Re-arranged Descending)**

| Duration (h) | 0–4 | 4–8 | 8–14 | 14–18 | 18–20 | 20–24 |
|--------------|-----|-----|------|-------|-------|-------|
| Load (MW) | 75 | 60 | 50 | 40 | 30 | 15–20 |

*(Detailed table: 75 MW → 4 h, 60 MW → 4 h, 50 MW → 3 h, 40 MW → 3 h, 30 MW → 2 h, 20 MW → 6 h, 15 MW → 2 h — total = 24 h.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Diversity Factor:**

$$
DF = \frac{120}{75} = 1.6
$$

**Demand Factor:**

$$
\text{DFac} = \frac{75}{100} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

**Plant Capacity Factor:**

$$
PCF = \frac{1020}{90 \times 24} = \frac{1020}{2160} = 0.4722 = 47.22\%
$$

**Plant Use Factor:**

$$
PUF = \frac{1020}{90 \times 20} = \frac{1020}{1800} = 0.5667 = 56.67\%
$$

---

### Question B2. **[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Internal Choice – Pattern B]** (14 Marks)

A power station has a maximum demand of 100 MW. The annual load factor is 0.5, diversity factor is 1.25, and demand factor is 0.8. The installed plant capacity is 120 MW, and the plant was operational for 6500 hours in the year.

**Find:**
(a) (7 Marks) Average load, total annual energy generated, sum of individual max demands, and connected load.
(b) (7 Marks) Reserve capacity, utilization factor, plant capacity factor, and plant use factor.

---

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Average Load:**

$$
L_{avg} = LF \times MD = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \; \text{MW}
$$

**Annual Energy Generated:**

$$
T = 8760 \; \text{h (1 year)}
$$

$$
E = L_{avg} \times T = 50 \times 8760 = 438000 \; \text{MWh}
$$

**Sum of Individual Max Demands:**

$$
\sum MD_i = DF \times MD = 1.25 \times 100 = 125 \; \text{MW}
$$

**Connected Load:**

$$
\text{Connected Load} = \frac{MD}{\text{DFac}} = \frac{100}{0.8} = 125 \; \text{MW}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Reserve Capacity:**

$$
\text{Reserve} = \text{Plant Capacity} - MD = 120 - 100 = 20 \; \text{MW}
$$

**Utilization Factor:**

$$
\text{UF} = \frac{MD}{\text{Plant Capacity}} = \frac{100}{120} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

**Plant Capacity Factor:**

$$
PCF = \frac{E}{\text{Cap} \times T} = \frac{438000}{120 \times 8760} = \frac{438000}{1051200} = 0.4167 = 41.67\%
$$

**Plant Use Factor:**

$$
PUF = \frac{E}{\text{Cap} \times t_{use}} = \frac{438000}{120 \times 6500} = \frac{438000}{780000} = 0.5615 = 56.15\%
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PCF and PUF Denominators:** PCF uses **total period** (T = 24 h or 8760 h); PUF uses **actual hours of use** ($t_{use}$). Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks easily.
> 2. **Forgetting the Energy Unit Conversion:** Energy from MW × h is in **MWh**, not kWh. If the problem gives kW, the energy is in kWh — don't mix.
> 3. **Demand Factor vs Diversity Factor Mix-up:** Demand Factor uses *Connected Load* (denominator), Diversity Factor uses *Sum of MDs* (numerator). These are often swapped by students under exam pressure.
> 4. **Missing the LDC Table:** Always provide a tabular re-arrangement of loads in descending order — the KTU examiner awards 1–2 marks for the LDC construction itself.
> 5. **Not Stating Units:** Always end with **kW, MW, kWh, MWh, or %** — missing units = 0.5 mark deduction per quantity.
> 6. **Load Factor Expressed as Decimal Only:** Convert to **percentage** for clarity. Some KTU answer keys explicitly want LF = 56.67%, not just 0.5667.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Load Curve** = Load vs *chronological time* (X = clock, Y = kW).
- [ ] **Load Duration Curve** = Load vs *duration* (loads sorted in **descending** order).
- [ ] **Area under the load curve = Energy generated** (in kWh or MWh).
- [ ] **Load Factor** = $\dfrac{L_{avg}}{MD}$ — always **≤ 1**.
- [ ] **Diversity Factor** = $\dfrac{\sum MD_i}{MD_{station}}$ — always **≥ 1**.
- [ ] **Demand Factor** = $\dfrac{MD}{\text{Connected Load}}$ — always **≤ 1**.
- [ ] **Plant Capacity Factor (PCF)** = $\dfrac{E}{\text{Cap} \times T}$ — uses **total period**.
- [ ] **Plant Use Factor (PUF)** = $\dfrac{E}{\text{Cap} \times t_{use}}$ — uses **operational hours only**.
- [ ] **PUF ≥ PCF** for the same data (always, without exception).
- [ ] **Reserve Capacity** = Plant Capacity − Max. Demand.
- [ ] **Utilization Factor** = $\dfrac{MD}{\text{Plant Capacity}}$.
- [ ] **One year** = **8760 hours** (always quote this for annual calculations).
- [ ] In a KTU numerical, **always compute Energy FIRST** — every other factor depends on it.
- [ ] **Express final answers in %** wherever possible (LF, PCF, PUF, Demand Factor).
- [ ] **Tabulate** the Load Duration Curve explicitly — it is a separately evaluable answer in KTU papers.
- [ ] Remember the **boundary constraints**: LF ≤ 1, DF ≥ 1, Demand Factor ≤ 1, PCF ≤ 1, PUF ≤ 1 — these are quick cross-checks to validate your numerical answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Transmission System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT503 - POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Transmission System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Power Transmission System?

A **Power Transmission System** is the high-voltage bulk transfer of electrical energy from generating stations (power plants) to substations located near load centers, from where distribution networks deliver power to end consumers. It forms the critical middle stage of the power system's three-tier architecture: **Generation → Transmission → Distribution**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "A Power Transmission System consists of conductors (overhead lines or underground cables) and associated equipment such as transformers, switching devices, and protective gear, designed to transmit large blocks of electrical power efficiently over long distances at high voltages (typically 66 kV to 765 kV and above in EHV/UHV class)."

### 1.2 Block Architecture of a Power System

The complete electrical power system is hierarchically structured as:

1. **Generation** (11 kV – 25 kV at the alternator terminals)
2. **Step-up Transformer** (raises voltage to 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV, etc.)
3. **Primary Transmission (EHV Bulk Power)** — long-distance, high-voltage corridors
4. **Step-down Substation (Primary Distribution)** — reduces to 66 kV / 33 kV
5. **Secondary Distribution** — 11 kV feeders
6. **Step-down Distribution Transformer** — 415 V / 240 V (3-phase / 1-phase to consumers)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Plumbing" of Electricity

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** Think of a transmission line as a **large-diameter water pipeline** that carries water from a far-away reservoir (power plant) to your city. To move large quantities efficiently, you must use a *wider pipe* and *higher pressure*. Similarly, electricity uses **higher voltage (pressure)** and **thicker conductors (larger pipe cross-section)** to minimize $I^2R$ losses over long distances. Voltage is "stepped up" at the source, transmitted at high pressure, and "stepped down" near your home — exactly analogous to pumping water up, transporting it, and then releasing it at usable pressure.

### 1.4 Why High-Voltage Transmission?

Power loss in a line is $P_{loss} = I^2 R$. For a given transmitted power $P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$, doubling the line voltage halves the line current, reducing $I^2R$ losses to **one-fourth**. This is the fundamental economic driver for EHV and UHV transmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Voltage Class Hierarchy (India – CEA Classification):**
> - **Low Voltage (LV):** ≤ 1 kV
> - **High Voltage (HV):** 11 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV
> - **Extra High Voltage (EHV):** 132 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV
> - **Ultra High Voltage (UHV):** ±800 kV HVDC, 1200 kV AC (pilot lines)

### 1.5 Components of a Transmission System

| Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Conductors** (ACSR/AAAC) | Carry the bulk current |
| **Insulators** (porcelain/glass/composite) | Isolate live conductors from support structure |
| **Towers/Poles** (lattice/monopole) | Mechanical support with safe ground clearance |
| **Earth Wire / Shield Wire** | Protects against direct lightning strikes |
| **Step-up / Step-down Transformers** | Voltage level conversion |
| **Circuit Breakers & Isolators** | Switching and protection |
| **Capacitor / Reactor Banks** | Reactive power management & voltage control |
| **CTs and PTs** | Measurement and protection |

### 1.6 Overhead (OH) vs Underground (UG) Transmission

| Criterion | Overhead (OH) | Underground (UG) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cost** | Lower (≈ 5–10× cheaper) | Very high (cables + civil works) |
| **Installation** | Quick, modular | Slow, requires trenching/boring |
| **Maintenance** | Easy visual inspection | Difficult fault localization |
| **Fault Rate** | Higher (exposed to storms, trees) | Lower |
| **Reactance** | Higher (large spacing) | Lower (close spacing) |
| **Surge Impedance** | ~400 Ω | ~40 Ω |
| **Usage** | Bulk, long-distance EHV | Urban, subsea crossings, sensitive zones |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transmission Line Parameters (The "Primary Constants")

Every transmission line, regardless of length, is characterized by four **uniformly distributed** parameters per unit length:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit (per km) | Physical Origin |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Resistance** | $R$ | $\Omega / \text{km}$ | Resistivity of conductor material |
| **Inductance** | $L$ | $\text{H} / \text{km}$ | Magnetic field linking the conductor |
| **Capacitance** | $C$ | $\text{F} / \text{km}$ | Potential difference between conductors |
| **Conductance** (leakance) | $G$ | $\text{S} / \text{km}$ | Leakage across insulators, corona |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** For short/medium lines, **$G$ and $C$ are often neglected** because they are either very small or have negligible effect. For long lines (> 250 km), **all four parameters must be considered as distributed elements** — not lumped.

---

## 2.2 Skin Effect and Proximity Effect

### Skin Effect
At AC, current density is non-uniform — it concentrates near the conductor surface, **increasing effective AC resistance** above its DC value.

$$R_{AC} = R_{DC} \cdot k_s \quad \text{where} \quad k_s > 1$$

The penetration depth depends on frequency, permeability, and conductivity.

### Proximity Effect
When two or more conductors carry AC in close proximity, the current distribution in each is distorted by the magnetic field of the other, further increasing effective resistance. Most pronounced in **bundled conductors** and **cables**.

> [!NOTE]
> In ACSR (Aluminium Conductor Steel Reinforced) conductors, steel is used for mechanical strength but contributes negligibly to conduction — current flows almost entirely in the outer aluminium strands, which also mitigates skin effect.

---

## 2.3 Inductance of a Transmission Line

### 2.3.1 Inductance of a Single Conductor (Two-Wire Line)

For a single-phase two-wire line carrying return current, the **loop inductance per conductor** is:

$$L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{r'}\right) \;\; \text{H/m per conductor}$$

where:
- $D$ = distance between conductor centers (m)
- $r' = r \cdot e^{-1/4} = 0.7788\, r$ = **effective radius** (GMR correction)

### 2.3.2 Inductance of Three-Phase Line (Symmetric Spacing)

$$L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{D_{eq}}{r'}\right) \;\; \text{H/m per phase}$$

$$D_{eq} = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} \cdot D_{bc} \cdot D_{ca}}$$

### 2.3.3 Inductance with Unsymmetric Spacing — Transposition

To equalize inductance across the three phases, line conductors are **transposed** (positions rotated periodically):

$$L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{\sqrt[3]{D_{ab}\,D_{bc}\,D_{ca}}}{r'}\right) \;\; \text{H/m per phase}$$

---

## 2.4 Capacitance of a Transmission Line

### 2.4.1 Capacitance Between Two Parallel Wires

$$C = \frac{\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/r)} \;\; \text{F/m per conductor}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

### 2.4.2 Capacitance of a Three-Phase Line (Transposed)

$$C_n = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(D_{eq}/r)} \;\; \text{F/m per phase (to neutral)}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | DC Resistance | $R_{DC} = \dfrac{\rho \, \ell}{A}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | AC Resistance (Skin) | $R_{AC} = R_{DC} \cdot k_s$ | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | GMR of solid round conductor | $r' = 0.7788\, r$ | m |
| 4 | Loop Inductance (1-phase) | $L = 4 \times 10^{-7} \ln(D/r')$ | H/m |
| 5 | Phase Inductance (3-φ) | $L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln(D_{eq}/r')$ | H/m |
| 6 | Phase Capacitance (3-φ) | $C_n = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D_{eq}/r)}$ | F/m |
| 7 | Surge Impedance | $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Surge Impedance Loading | $\text{SIL} = \dfrac{V_L^2}{Z_c}$ | MW |
| 9 | Propagation Constant | $\gamma = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$ | 1/km |
| 10 | Characteristic Impedance | $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | Voltage Regulation | $\%\text{VR} = \dfrac{\vert V_S\vert - \vert V_R\vert}{\vert V_R\vert} \times 100$ | % |
| 12 | Transmission Efficiency | $\%\eta = \dfrac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100$ | % |
| 13 | ABCD Parameter (Lossless) | $A = D = \cos\beta\ell,\; B = j Z_c \sin\beta\ell$ | — |
| 14 | Real Power Flow | $P_R = \dfrac{V_S V_R}{Z_c} \sin\delta$ | W |
| 15 | Reactive Power Flow | $Q_R = \dfrac{V_S V_R \cos\delta - V_R^2}{Z_c}$ | VAr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)** concept is the most practical single number a transmission engineer uses. A line loaded *below* SIL behaves like a **capacitor** (Ferranti effect — receiving-end voltage rises), while loaded *above* SIL it behaves like an **inductor** (voltage drops). System operators aim to keep EHV lines near SIL for best voltage profile and minimum losses.

---

## 2.6 Classification of Transmission Lines by Length

| Category | Length | Parameters Considered | Model |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Short line** | $< 80$ km | $R, L$ only | Nominal-T / Nominal-π / End-condenser |
| **Medium line** | 80 – 250 km | $R, L, C$ (lumped, half at one end) | Nominal-T / Nominal-π |
| **Long line** | $> 250$ km | All four, **distributed** | ABCD constants / Hyperbolic model |

---

## 2.7 ABCD Generalized Constants

The two-port network representation of any transmission line obeys:

$$V_S = A V_R + B I_R$$
$$I_S = C V_R + D I_R$$

For all line models, the fundamental identity is $AD - BC = 1$.

For a **lossless long line** ($R = G = 0$):

$$A = D = \cos(\beta \ell), \quad B = j Z_c \sin(\beta \ell), \quad C = j \frac{\sin(\beta \ell)}{Z_c}$$

where $\beta = \omega \sqrt{LC}$ is the phase constant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inductance of a Single Isolated Long Straight Conductor

### Starting Point — Magnetic Field Outside an Infinite Conductor

By **Ampere's Law**, the magnetic flux density at radial distance $x$ from the center of an isolated long straight conductor carrying current $I$ is:

$$B_x = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi x}$$

### Flux Linkage Between $r_1$ and $D$ (per unit length)

The flux per unit length linking the conductor between the conductor surface (at $r_1$) and a far point $D$ is:

$$\phi = \int_{r_1}^{D} B_x \, dx = \int_{r_1}^{D} \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi x} \, dx = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{r_1}\right)$$

### Internal Flux Linkage (Inside Conductor)

Inside a solid round conductor of radius $r_1$, the magnetic field is non-uniform. Carrying out integration (assuming uniform current density):

$$\phi_{int} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{8 \pi} \quad \text{(per unit length)}$$

This can be modeled as flux linking the conductor from $r_1$ to an **equivalent radius**:

$$\frac{\mu_0 I}{8 \pi} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r'}\right) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r' = r_1 \cdot e^{-1/4} = 0.7788 \, r_1$$

### Total Inductance Per Conductor (Two-Wire Loop)

Loop inductance per unit length = external flux (both conductors) + internal flux (both):

$$L_{loop} = \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{r'}\right) \right] \;\;\text{H/m}$$

Converting $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$:

$$\boxed{L = 4 \times 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{r'}\right) \;\;\text{H/m per conductor}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacitance of a Two-Wire Line

### Potential of Conductor 1 (with Image Method)

The potential of conductor 1 with respect to a neutral plane midway between the two conductors (each of radius $r$, spacing $D$) is:

$$V_1 = \frac{1}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0} \left[ q_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{r}\right) - q_2 \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{D}\right) \right]$$

For a balanced two-wire line, $q_1 = -q_2 = q$. The potential difference between conductors:

$$V_1 - V_2 = 2 V_1 = \frac{q}{\pi \varepsilon_0} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{r}\right)$$

### Capacitance Between Conductors

$$C = \frac{q}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/r)} \;\;\text{F/m}$$

Substituting $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{0.0278}{\log_{10}(D/r)} \;\;\text{μF/km per conductor}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **"capacitance to neutral"** formulation commonly used in per-phase analysis of three-phase lines.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Performance of a Short Transmission Line

### Equivalent Circuit
A short line has only series $R$ and $X = \omega L$. Shunt $C$ and $G$ are neglected.

Sending end: $V_S = V_R + I(R + jX)$
Sending end current: $I_S = I_R = I$ (same through line)

### Phasor Diagram
With $V_R$ as reference, current $I$ lags by $\cos^{-1}\phi_R$. The voltage drop $IZ$ has components $IR\cos\phi_R + IX\sin\phi_R$ (in-phase) and $IX\cos\phi_R - IR\sin\phi_R$ (quadrature).

### Approximate Voltage Regulation

$$|V_S| \approx V_R + I(R\cos\phi_R + X\sin\phi_R)$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{VR} \approx \frac{I(R\cos\phi_R + X\sin\phi_R)}{V_R} \times 100}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Sign Convention:** This gives **+ve regulation** (lagging load). For leading PF loads (synchronous motors, capacitors), the regulation can be **negative** (Ferranti-like behavior even on short lines).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ABCD Constants for a Long Transmission Line

### Starting Differential Equations
Let $z = R + j\omega L$ and $y = G + j\omega C$ be the series impedance and shunt admittance per unit length. Let $x$ be distance measured from the receiving end.

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = z I, \quad \frac{dI}{dx} = y V$$

### Differentiating and Combining

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = z \frac{dI}{dx} = z y V = \gamma^2 V$$

where $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$ is the **propagation constant**.

The general solution is:

$$V(x) = A_1 e^{\gamma x} + A_2 e^{-\gamma x}$$

$$I(x) = \frac{1}{Z_c}(A_1 e^{\gamma x} - A_2 e^{-\gamma x})$$

with $Z_c = \sqrt{z/y}$ = characteristic impedance.

### Applying Boundary Conditions

At $x = 0$ (receiving end): $V(0) = V_R$ and $I(0) = I_R$.

$$V_R = A_1 + A_2, \quad I_R Z_c = A_1 - A_2$$

Solving:

$$A_1 = \frac{V_R + I_R Z_c}{2}, \quad A_2 = \frac{V_R - I_R Z_c}{2}$$

### Final Hyperbolic Form (at $x = \ell$, sending end)

$$V_S = V_R \cosh(\gamma \ell) + I_R Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell)$$

$$I_S = I_R \cosh(\gamma \ell) + \frac{V_R}{Z_c} \sinh(\gamma \ell)$$

Hence the ABCD parameters:

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} A &= \cosh(\gamma \ell) \\ B &= Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell) \\ C &= \dfrac{1}{Z_c} \sinh(\gamma \ell) \\ D &= \cosh(\gamma \ell) \end{aligned}}$$

with $AD - BC = \cosh^2(\gamma\ell) - \sinh^2(\gamma\ell) = 1$ (verified).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Line Models at a Glance

| Model | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | When to Use |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Short Line** | $1$ | $Z$ | $0$ | $1$ | $< 80$ km |
| **Nominal-T** | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | $Z\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$ | $Y$ | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | Medium line |
| **Nominal-π** | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | $Z$ | $Y\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$ | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | Medium line |
| **Long Line (Exact)** | $\cosh(\gamma\ell)$ | $Z_c\sinh(\gamma\ell)$ | $\dfrac{\sinh(\gamma\ell)}{Z_c}$ | $\cosh(\gamma\ell)$ | All lengths |
| **Lossless Long Line** | $\cos(\beta\ell)$ | $jZ_c\sin(\beta\ell)$ | $j\dfrac{\sin(\beta\ell)}{Z_c}$ | $\cos(\beta\ell)$ | $R=G=0$ |

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 200 km, 3-phase, 50 Hz transmission line has $R = 0.15\,\Omega/\text{km}$, $L = 1.4\,\text{mH/km}$, $C = 9.5\,\text{nF/km}$. Compute $Z_c$ and $\gamma$ using the long-line model. If $V_R = 220\,\text{kV (L-L)}$ and $P_R = 50$ MW at 0.85 PF lagging, find $V_S$ and regulation.

**Given (per phase):**
$$z = R + j\omega L = 0.15 + j(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1.4\times 10^{-3}) = 0.15 + j\,0.4398 \;\;\Omega/\text{km}$$
$$y = j\omega C = j(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 9.5\times 10^{-9}) = j\,2.9845\times 10^{-6} \;\;\text{S/km}$$

**Step 1: Characteristic Impedance**
$$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{z}{y}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.15 + j\,0.4398}{j\,2.9845\times 10^{-6}}}$$

Compute numerator magnitude: $\vert z\vert = \sqrt{0.15^2 + 0.4398^2} = 0.4647$, $\angle z = \tan^{-1}(0.4398/0.15) = 71.15°$

So $z = 0.4647\angle 71.15°$, $y = 2.9845 \times 10^{-6} \angle 90°$

$$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{0.4647}{2.9845\times 10^{-6}}}\angle\frac{71.15° - 90°}{2} = \sqrt{155,720} \angle -9.425°$$
$$Z_c = 394.6 \angle -9.43° \;\;\Omega$$

**Step 2: Propagation Constant**
$$\gamma = \sqrt{zy} = \sqrt{0.4647 \times 2.9845\times 10^{-6}} \angle\frac{71.15° + 90°}{2}$$
$$\gamma = \sqrt{1.3870 \times 10^{-6}} \angle 80.57° = 1.178\times 10^{-3} \angle 80.57° \;\;\text{1/km}$$
$$\gamma \ell = (1.178\times 10^{-3})(200)\angle 80.57° = 0.2356 \angle 80.57° = 0.0387 + j\,0.2324$$

**Step 3: Hyperbolic Functions**
$$\cosh(\gamma\ell) = \cosh(0.0387 + j\,0.2324)$$
$$= \cosh(0.0387)\cos(0.2324) + j\sinh(0.0387)\sin(0.2324)$$
$$= (1.00075)(0.9731) + j(0.0387)(0.2303)$$
$$A = D = 0.9738 + j\,0.00891$$

$$\sinh(\gamma\ell) = \sinh(0.0387)\cos(0.2324) + j\cosh(0.0387)\sin(0.2324)$$
$$= (0.0387)(0.9731) + j(1.00075)(0.2303)$$
$$= 0.03767 + j\,0.2305$$

**Step 4: ABCD Parameters**
$$B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma\ell) = 394.6\angle-9.43° \times (0.03767 + j\,0.2305)$$
$$|B| = 394.6 \times 0.2336 = 92.18,\; \angle B = -9.43° + 80.45° = 71.02°$$
$$B = 30.04 + j\,87.16 \;\;\Omega$$

**Step 5: Receiving End Quantities (per phase)**
$$V_R = \frac{220{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} = 127{,}017 \;\text{V (phase)}$$
$$I_R = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 220{,}000 \times 0.85} = 154.4 \;\text{A} \angle -31.79°$$

**Step 6: Sending End Voltage**
$$V_S = A V_R + B I_R$$
$$V_S = (0.9738 + j\,0.00891)(127{,}017\angle 0°) + (30.04 + j\,87.16)(154.4\angle -31.79°)$$

Term 1: $127{,}017 \times 0.9740 = 123{,}733 \;\text{V (with tiny quadrature)}$

Term 2: $B I_R = 92.18\angle 71.02° \times 154.4\angle -31.79° = 14{,}233 \angle 39.23°$
$$= 14{,}233(0.7760 + j\,0.6308) = 11{,}045 + j\,8{,}978 \;\text{V}$$

Total: $V_S = (123{,}733 + 11{,}045) + j(2,535 + 8{,}978) = 134{,}778 + j\,11{,}513 \;\text{V}$

$$|V_S| = \sqrt{134{,}778^2 + 11{,}513^2} = 135{,}268 \;\text{V (phase)}$$
$$V_S(\text{L-L}) = 135{,}268 \times \sqrt{3} = 234.3 \;\text{kV}$$

**Step 7: Voltage Regulation**
$$\%\text{VR} = \frac{234.3 - 220}{220} \times 100 = 6.5\%$$

**Step 8: Efficiency**
$$P_{loss} = 3 |I_R|^2 R_{total} = 3 \times (154.4)^2 \times (0.15 \times 200) = 2.146 \;\text{MW}$$
$$\%\eta = \frac{50}{50 + 2.146} \times 100 = 95.9\%$$
