# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define **Surge Impedance (Characteristic Impedance) of a Transmission Line**. What is the **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)**? For a 400 kV line, what is the typical value of SIL?

**Model Answer:**

> **Surge Impedance** ($Z_c$) is the natural impedance a long transmission line presents to a travelling voltage wave. It is the square root of the ratio of the series impedance per km to the shunt admittance per km:
>
> $$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}} \;\;\Omega$$
>
> For a lossless line ($R = G = 0$), $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$, which is purely resistive.
>
> **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)** is the power delivered by the line to a load equal to $Z_c$ at rated voltage:
>
> $$\text{SIL} = \frac{V_L^2}{Z_c}$$
>
> For a 400 kV, 50 Hz line with $Z_c \approx 400\,\Omega$:
>
> $$\text{SIL} = \frac{(400 \times 10^3)^2}{400} = 400 \;\text{MW}$$
>
> Thus, **the typical SIL of a 400 kV line is about 400 MW**. Operation near SIL gives the flattest voltage profile and minimum line losses.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the **Ferranti Effect** in long EHV transmission lines. Under what condition does it become significant?

**Model Answer:**

> The **Ferranti Effect** is the phenomenon where the **receiving-end voltage of a long transmission line exceeds the sending-end voltage** when the line is lightly loaded (or unloaded).
>
> **Cause:** The shunt capacitance of a long line draws a leading current that produces a **voltage rise** along the series inductance of the line.
>
> **Mathematical basis:** For a lossless line, $V_S = V_R \cos(\beta\ell) + j I_R Z_c \sin(\beta\ell)$. With $I_R = 0$ (open circuit), $V_S = V_R \cos(\beta\ell)$. Since $\cos(\beta\ell) < 1$ for $\beta\ell > 0$, this gives:
>
> $$\frac{V_R}{V_S} = \frac{1}{\cos(\beta\ell)} > 1$$
>
> The receiving-end voltage is therefore **higher** than the sending-end.
>
> **When significant:** For lines longer than **250 km** at 50 Hz, the effect becomes appreciable (5–10% voltage rise on 400 kV lines). It is **most pronounced under no-load or very-light-load conditions**, especially on long EHV and UHV lines. Mitigation: install **shunt reactors** at the receiving end to absorb the charging MVAR.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]** A three-phase, 50 Hz, 250 km transmission line has the following per-phase parameters:

- $R = 0.12 \;\Omega/\text{km}$
- $L = 1.3 \;\text{mH/km}$
- $C = 10 \;\text{nF/km}$
- $G = 0$ (negligible)

The line delivers **80 MW** at **0.9 power factor lagging** to a receiving-end load at **220 kV (line-to-line)**. Using the **long-line model (ABCD parameters)**:

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the characteristic impedance $Z_c$, propagation constant $\gamma$, and the ABCD parameters.

**(b) [7 marks]** Determine the sending-end voltage, sending-end current, voltage regulation, and transmission efficiency.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Compute $z$ and $y$ per km**

$$z = R + j\omega L = 0.12 + j(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1.3 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.12 + j\,0.4084 \;\Omega/\text{km}$$

$$\vert z \vert = \sqrt{0.12^2 + 0.4084^2} = 0.4257, \quad \theta_z = \tan^{-1}(0.4084/0.12) = 73.61°$$

$$y = j\omega C = j(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-9}) = j\,3.1416 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{S/km} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-6} \angle 90° \;\text{S/km}$$

**Step 2: Characteristic Impedance**

$$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{z}{y}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.4257 \angle 73.61°}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6} \angle 90°}}$$

$$Z_c = \sqrt{1.3550 \times 10^{5}} \angle \frac{73.61° - 90°}{2} = 368.1 \angle -8.20° \;\Omega$$

**Step 3: Propagation Constant**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{zy} = \sqrt{0.4257 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-6}} \angle \frac{73.61° + 90°}{2}$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{1.3373 \times 10^{-6}} \angle 81.81° = 1.1565 \times 10^{-3} \angle 81.81° \;\text{1/km}$$

$$\gamma \ell = (1.1565 \times 10^{-3})(250) \angle 81.81° = 0.2891 \angle 81.81°$$

$$\gamma \ell = 0.2891 \cos 81.81° + j\,0.2891 \sin 81.81° = 0.0410 + j\,0.2862$$

**Step 4: Hyperbolic Functions**

$$\cosh(\gamma\ell) = \cosh(0.0410)\cos(0.2862) + j\sinh(0.0410)\sin(0.2862)$$
$$= (1.00084)(0.9592) + j(0.0410)(0.2823) = 0.9600 + j\,0.01158$$

$$A = D = 0.9600 + j\,0.01158$$

$$\sinh(\gamma\ell) = \sinh(0.0410)\cos(0.2862) + j\cosh(0.0410)\sin(0.2862)$$
$$= (0.0410)(0.9592) + j(1.00084)(0.2823) = 0.03933 + j\,0.2825$$

**Step 5: B and C parameters**

$$B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma\ell) = 368.1\angle-8.20° \times 0.2842\angle 82.09° = 104.6\angle 73.89°\;\Omega$$

$$B = 29.18 + j\,100.42\;\Omega$$

$$C = \frac{\sinh(\gamma\ell)}{Z_c} = \frac{0.2842\angle 82.09°}{368.1\angle-8.20°} = 7.720 \times 10^{-4}\angle 90.29°\;\text{S}$$

$$C = -2.51 \times 10^{-6} + j\,7.72 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{S}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Receiving-end per-phase voltage and current**

$$V_R = \frac{220{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} = 127{,}017\angle 0°\;\text{V (phase)}$$

$$I_R = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 220{,}000 \times 0.9} = 233.0\angle -25.84°\;\text{A}$$

**Step 2: Sending-end voltage**

$$V_S = A V_R + B I_R$$

$$= (0.9600 + j\,0.01158)(127{,}017) + (29.18 + j\,100.42)(233.0\angle -25.84°)$$

Term 1: $V_{R\text{-term}} = 121{,}936 + j\,1{,}471\;\text{V}$

Term 2: Magnitude of $B I_R$ = $104.6 \times 233.0 = 24{,}372$ V, angle = $73.89° - 25.84° = 48.05°$
$$= 24{,}372(\cos 48.05° + j\sin 48.05°) = 16{,}302 + j\,18{,}132\;\text{V}$$

Total: $V_S = (121{,}936 + 16{,}302) + j(1{,}471 + 18{,}132) = 138{,}238 + j\,19{,}603\;\text{V}$

$$|V_S| = \sqrt{138{,}238^2 + 19{,}603^2} = 139{,}622\;\text{V (phase)}$$

$$V_S(\text{L-L}) = 139{,}622 \times \sqrt{3} = 241.83\;\text{kV}$$

**Step 3: Sending-end current**

$$I_S = C V_R + D I_R$$

$$= (7.720 \times 10^{-4}\angle 90.29°)(127{,}017) + (0.9600 + j\,0.01158)(233.0\angle -25.84°)$$

Term 1: $98.06\angle 90.29° = -1.07 + j\,98.05\;\text{A}$

Term 2: $D \cdot I_R$ magnitude = $0.9601 \times 233.0 = 223.7$, angle = $0.66° - 25.84° = -25.18°$
$$= 223.7 \cos(-25.18°) + j\,223.7 \sin(-25.18°) = 202.5 - j\,95.2\;\text{A}$$

Total: $I_S = (202.5 - 1.07) + j(-95.2 + 98.05) = 201.4 + j\,2.85\;\text{A}$

$$|I_S| = 201.4\;\text{A}, \quad \angle I_S = 0.81°$$

**Step 4: Voltage Regulation**

$$\%\text{VR} = \frac{V_{S(\text{L-L})} - V_{R(\text{L-L})}}{V_{R(\text{L-L})}} \times 100 = \frac{241.83 - 220}{220} \times 100 = 9.92\%$$

**Step 5: Transmission Efficiency**

$$P_R = 80\;\text{MW (given)}$$

$$P_S = \sqrt{3} V_S I_S \cos\phi_S = \sqrt{3}(241{,}830)(201.4)\cos(0.81° - 0.81°)$$

Wait — let me recompute: $P_S = 3 \text{Re}\{V_S I_S^*\}$

$$V_S I_S^* = (138{,}238 + j\,19{,}603)(201.4 - j\,2.85)$$
$$= 138{,}238 \times 201.4 + 19{,}603 \times 2.85 + j(\text{imag})$$
$$= 27{,}841{,}153 + 55{,}869 + j(\cdots) = 27{,}897{,}022 + j(\cdots)$$

$$P_S = 3 \times 27{,}897{,}022 = 83.69\;\text{MW}$$

$$\%\eta = \frac{80}{83.69} \times 100 = 95.6\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Prevention:**
> 1. **Do NOT use the short-line or nominal-π formula for a 250 km line.** This is a *long line* — the question explicitly asks for the long-line (ABCD) model.
> 2. Always work in **per-phase values** for ABCD analysis. Convert L-L voltages by dividing by $\sqrt{3}$.
> 3. **Maintain consistent reference phasor:** Take $V_R$ as the reference ($0°$). All other quantities must be expressed relative to it.
> 4. **Use the identity $AD - BC = 1$** to verify your ABCD computation — this catches sign and magnitude errors.
> 5. **Efficiency** must be computed as $P_R / P_S$ using the **sending-end** active power, not by $I^2R$ approximation alone (which ignores shunt losses on long lines).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]** A 100 km, 3-phase, 50 Hz overhead transmission line has:
- $R = 0.25 \;\Omega/\text{km}$, $L = 1.5 \;\text{mH/km}$, $C = 8.5 \;\text{nF/km}$

**(a) [7 marks]** Model the line using the **nominal-π representation** and derive expressions for the sending-end voltage and current in terms of receiving-end quantities.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the line delivers **40 MW at 0.85 PF lagging** to a load at **132 kV (L-L)**, find the sending-end voltage, current, regulation, and efficiency using the nominal-π model.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Total line parameters**

$$Z = (R + j\omega L) \cdot \ell = [0.25 + j(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-3})] \times 100$$
$$= (0.25 + j\,0.4712)(100) = 25 + j\,47.12\;\Omega$$

$$Y = j\omega C \cdot \ell = j(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 8.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times 100 = j\,2.670 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{S}$$

**Step 2: Nominal-π ABCD parameters**

$$A = D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z, \quad C = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$

Compute $YZ$:
$$YZ = (j\,2.670 \times 10^{-4})(25 + j\,47.12) = j\,6.675 \times 10^{-3} - 0.01258$$
$$YZ = -0.01258 + j\,6.675 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$A = D = 1 + \frac{-0.01258 + j\,6.675 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 0.9937 + j\,3.338 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\vert A \vert = 0.9937, \quad \angle A = 0.192°$$

$$B = Z = 25 + j\,47.12 = 53.35\angle 62.04°\;\Omega$$

$$C = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) = (j\,2.670 \times 10^{-4})\left(1 + \frac{-0.01258 + j\,6.675 \times 10^{-3}}{4}\right)$$

$$1 + \frac{YZ}{4} = 0.9969 + j\,1.669 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$C = (j\,2.670 \times 10^{-4})(0.9969 + j\,1.669 \times 10^{-3}) = -4.456 \times 10^{-7} + j\,2.661 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{S}$$

**Step 3: Sending-end expressions**

$$\boxed{V_S = A V_R + B I_R = \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)V_R + Z I_R}$$

$$\boxed{I_S = C V_R + D I_R = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) V_R + \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) I_R}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Receiving-end values (per phase)**

$$V_R = \frac{132{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} = 76{,}210\angle 0°\;\text{V}$$

$$I_R = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 132{,}000 \times 0.85} = 205.6\angle -31.79°\;\text{A}$$

**Step 2: Sending-end voltage**

$$V_S = A V_R + B I_R$$

Term 1: $A V_R = (0.9937 + j\,3.338 \times 10^{-3})(76{,}210) = 75{,}738 + j\,254\;\text{V}$

Term 2: $B I_R = 53.35\angle 62.04° \times 205.6\angle -31.79° = 10{,}969\angle 30.25°$
$$= 9{,}475 + j\,5{,}527\;\text{V}$$

Total: $V_S = 85{,}213 + j\,5{,}781 = 85{,}409\angle 3.88°\;\text{V (phase)}$

$$V_S(\text{L-L}) = 85{,}409 \times \sqrt{3} = 147.94\;\text{kV}$$

**Step 3: Sending-end current**

$$I_S = C V_R + D I_R$$

Term 1: $C V_R = (-4.456 \times 10^{-7} + j\,2.661 \times 10^{-4})(76{,}210)$
$$= -0.03395 + j\,20.28\;\text{A}$$

Term 2: $D I_R = (0.9937 + j\,3.338 \times 10^{-3})(205.6\angle -31.79°)$
$$D \approx 0.9937\angle 0.192°, \text{ so } D I_R = 204.3\angle -31.60°$$
$$= 173.9 - j\,107.1\;\text{A}$$

Total: $I_S = (173.9 - 0.034) + j(-107.1 + 20.28) = 173.9 - j\,86.8\;\text{A}$

$$|I_S| = 194.4\;\text{A}, \quad \angle I_S = -26.55°$$

**Step 4: Voltage Regulation**

$$\%\text{VR} = \frac{147.94 - 132}{132} \times 100 = 12.08\%$$

**Step 5: Efficiency**

$$P_S = 3 \text{Re}\{V_S I_S^*\} = 3 \text{Re}\{(85{,}213 + j\,5{,}781)(173.9 + j\,86.8)\}$$
$$= 3 (85{,}213 \times 173.9 - 5{,}781 \times 86.8)$$
$$= 3(14{,}823{,}547 - 501{,}791) = 3 \times 14{,}321{,}756 = 42.97\;\text{MW}$$

$$\%\eta = \frac{40}{42.97} \times 100 = 93.1\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Prevention:**
> 1. For a **100 km line**, both **nominal-T and nominal-π** are acceptable per KTU syllabus — but the question specifies π. **Read carefully**.
> 2. The **shunt admittance is split equally** at the two ends in the π model. Do NOT place it all in the middle.
> 3. The **active power transmission loss includes the $I^2R$ component AND the small charging contribution**. Always use $P_S = 3 \text{Re}\{V_S I_S^*\}$ for accuracy.
> 4. **Power factor angle sign:** "Lagging" → current **lags** voltage → $\phi = -\cos^{-1}(0.85) = -31.79°$.
> 5. The **regulation is positive (12%)** for a lagging load. If the load were leading (over-excited synchronous motor), regulation could be **negative**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### Core Concept Pillars
- ☐ A transmission line is characterized by **four distributed parameters**: $R$, $L$, $C$, $G$ per unit length.
- ☐ **Short line** (< 80 km): $R$ and $L$ (series) only; $C$, $G$ neglected.
- ☐ **Medium line** (80–250 km): lumped $R$, $L$, $C$ → Nominal-T or Nominal-π.
- ☐ **Long line** (> 250 km): distributed parameters → **hyperbolic ABCD** model mandatory.
- ☐ **Skin effect** raises $R_{AC}$ above $R_{DC}$; depends on $\sqrt{f}$ at high frequencies.
- ☐ **GMR** of a solid round conductor is $r' = 0.7788\,r$.
- ☐ **GMD** (Geometric Mean Distance) for symmetric 3-phase: $D_{eq} = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} D_{bc} D_{ca}}$.
- ☐ Inductance $\uparrow$ with conductor spacing, $\downarrow$ with conductor radius.
- ☐ Capacitance $\uparrow$ with conductor radius, $\downarrow$ with spacing.
- ☐ **Surge Impedance** $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$ for lossless line (typically 400–500 Ω for OH, 40 Ω for UG).
- ☐ **SIL** = $V_L^2 / Z_c$ — the natural loading of a line.
- ☐ **Ferranti Effect** → receiving-end voltage rises on light load (or no load).
- ☐ **Voltage Regulation** ≈ $[IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi]/V_R$ (short-line approximation).
- ☐ **Bundled conductors** reduce corona, lower $Z_c$, raise SIL.
- ☐ **Always verify** $AD - BC = 1$ for ABCD parameters.

### Key Numerical Values to Memorize
- ☐ $1/(4\pi\varepsilon_0) = 9 \times 10^9$ N·m²/C²
- ☐ $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- ☐ $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- ☐ $50\,\text{Hz} \Rightarrow \omega = 314.16$ rad/s
- ☐ $60\,\text{Hz} \Rightarrow \omega = 376.99$ rad/s (for international problems)
- ☐ Conversion: $C(\text{F/m}) = 0.0278/\log_{10}(D/r)$ μF/km

### Common Student Mistakes (Avoid in KTU Exam)
- ☐ Mixing per-phase and line-to-line quantities.
- ☐ Forgetting to take $V_R$ as reference phasor.
- ☐ Using nominal-π for a 400 km line (this is a long line!).
- ☐ Sign errors in $\cos\phi$ and $\sin\phi$ for leading vs. lagging loads.
- ☐ Reporting $Z_c$ as purely real — it has a small angle due to $R$ and $G$.
- ☐ Using the same line length for all parameter calculations — make sure to use **total line $\ell$**, not per-km.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT503 - POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Electrical Model of a Transmission Line?

In power system analysis, a **Transmission Line** is never treated as an ideal wire. Because conductors are long (often hundreds of kilometers), they exhibit **distributed electrical parameters** along their length: resistance, inductance, capacitance, and (to a lesser extent) conductance to ground.

The **Electrical Model** of a transmission line is the **mathematical equivalent circuit** that mathematically replicates the steady-state voltage and current behavior of a real overhead line at the sending end and receiving end, **without physically drawing every infinitesimal segment of wire**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCEOT503, Module 2):**
> *"The electrical model of a transmission line is its representation using lumped (R, L, C) or distributed parameters to analyze the relationship between sending-end and receiving-end voltage, current, power, and efficiency for short, medium, and long lines."*

## 1.2 Why Do We Need a Model?

A transmission line operating at **50 Hz** in Kerala/Karnataka typically spans **80–400 km**. At such lengths, the line cannot be considered a "short" lumped circuit. The distributed nature of parameters causes:
- **Voltage drop** along the line
- **Reactive power** consumption
- **Ferranti effect** (line energization)
- **Surge impedance loading** behavior
- **Standing waves** under load variation

Hence, an **electrical model** is essential to predict performance *before* construction.

## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy 🚰

> **Intuition — The "Garden Hose Pipeline" Analogy:**

Imagine pumping water through a very long, thin, slightly leaky garden hose:
- The **friction inside the hose** mimics series **Resistance (R)** — it reduces flow (current) and pressure (voltage).
- The **inertia of water** moving back and forth mimics series **Inductance (L)** — it opposes sudden changes.
- The **elasticity of the hose walls** (which expand and store water) mimics shunt **Capacitance (C)** — it stores charge.
- The **slow leakage** through microscopic holes mimics shunt **Conductance (G)** — it represents losses to ground (corona, leakage).

If the hose is **short (< 80 km)**, you only worry about friction (R) and inertia (L) → **Short Line Model**.
If the hose is **medium (80–250 km)**, the elasticity (C) becomes a "lumped bulge" in the middle → **Nominal-π or Nominal-T Model**.
If the hose is **very long (> 250 km)**, every inch of the hose has these properties → **Long Line Model with Distributed Parameters**.

> [!NOTE]
> The classification limits (80 km and 250 km) are KTU-standard benchmark values for overhead lines operating at 50 Hz. For **cables**, these limits are even shorter due to higher capacitance.

## 1.4 The Four Line Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Nature | Unit | Represents |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resistance | $R$ | Series (along conductor) | Ω/km | Conductor $I^2R$ losses |
| Inductance | $L$ | Series (along conductor) | H/km | Magnetic flux linkage |
| Capacitance | $C$ | Shunt (conductor to ground) | F/km | Electrostatic charge storage |
| Conductance | $G$ | Shunt (across insulation) | S/km | Leakage + corona losses |

> [!TIP]
> **For ESE (End Semester) — remember the order:** R and L are **longitudinal** (series). C and G are **transverse** (shunt). Always draw them in the equivalent circuit in this orientation.

## 1.5 Classification of Transmission Lines by Length

| Classification | Length Range | Modeling Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Short Line** | Up to **80 km** | Lumped **R + jX** only; C and G ignored |
| **Medium Line** | **80 km – 250 km** | Lumped **Nominal-π** or **Nominal-T** (R, L, C lumped) |
| **Long Line** | **Above 250 km** | **Distributed parameter** model (rigorous solution) |
| **DC Line** | Any length | Special case — only R considered, no X, no C |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Short Transmission Line Model

### 2.1.1 Equivalent Circuit
A short line is represented as a simple **series impedance** only.

$$Z = R + jX_L$$

where $X_L = 2\pi f L$.

- **Sending End:** $V_S, I_S$
- **Receiving End:** $V_R, I_R$

### 2.1.2 Phasor Relationships
Because the circuit is purely series, the **current is the same** at both ends:

$$I_S = I_R = I$$

The sending-end voltage equals the receiving-end voltage plus the drop across the impedance:

$$V_S = V_R + I \cdot Z$$

### 2.1.3 Phasor Diagram
- $V_R$ is taken as the reference phasor.
- Current $I$ lags $V_R$ by the load power factor angle $\phi_R$.
- The drop $IZ$ leads $I$ by angle $\theta$ (where $\tan\theta = X_L/R$).
- $V_S$ is the phasor sum: $V_S = V_R + I(R\cos\phi_R + X_L\sin\phi_R) + jI(X_L\cos\phi_R - R\sin\phi_R)$.

### 2.1.4 Approximate Voltage Regulation
For KTU problems, the **approximate** voltage regulation formula is most often used:

$$\% \text{Regulation} = \frac{|V_S| - |V_R|}{|V_R|} \times 100 \approx \frac{IR\cos\phi_R + IX_L\sin\phi_R}{|V_R|} \times 100$$

## 2.2 The Medium Transmission Line Model

### 2.2.1 Nominal-π Model
The total line capacitance is **lumped and divided equally** at the sending and receiving ends.

- Total series impedance: $Z = R + jX_L$ (lumped in the middle).
- Each shunt branch: $Y/2 = (G + jB_C)/2$ where $B_C = \omega C$.

**Phasor equations for Nominal-π:**

$$I_C = V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2}$$

$$I_{line} = I_R + I_C$$

$$V_S = V_R + I_{line} \cdot Z$$

$$I_S = I_{line} + V_S \cdot \frac{Y}{2}$$

### 2.2.2 Nominal-T Model
The total line inductance and resistance are **split into two equal halves**, and the total shunt capacitance is **lumped in the middle**.

- Each series branch: $Z/2 = (R + jX_L)/2$.
- Shunt branch in middle: $Y = G + jB_C$.

**Phasor equations for Nominal-T:**

$$I_{line} = I_R$$

$$V_m = V_R + I_R \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$

$$I_C = V_m \cdot Y$$

$$I_S = I_{line} + I_C = I_R + I_C$$

$$V_S = V_m + I_S \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Both Nominal-π and Nominal-T yield *almost identical* results for a medium line. The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 emphasizes that Nominal-π is preferred because it requires only **sending-end current calculation**, making it computationally lighter.

## 2.3 The Long Transmission Line Model (Distributed Parameters)

### 2.3.1 Concept
For lines > 250 km, the parameters are **uniformly distributed**. The line is described by the **telegraphist's equations**:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = z \cdot I$$

$$\frac{dI}{dx} = y \cdot V$$

where:
- $z = r + j\omega L$ (series impedance per unit length, Ω/km)
- $y = g + j\omega C$ (shunt admittance per unit length, S/km)

### 2.3.2 Characteristic (Surge) Impedance

$$Z_C = \sqrt{\frac{z}{y}} = \sqrt{\frac{r + j\omega L}{g + j\omega C}}$$

For a **lossless** approximation ($r=0, g=0$):

$$Z_C = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $Z_C$ is **purely resistive** for a lossless line and is independent of line length. For overhead lines, typical value ≈ **400 Ω** (range 350–600 Ω). For cables, ≈ **40–60 Ω**.

### 2.3.3 Propagation Constant

$$\gamma = \sqrt{z \cdot y} = \alpha + j\beta$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = **Attenuation constant** (nepers/km)
- $\beta$ = **Phase constant** (radians/km)

### 2.3.4 ABCD Parameters (Generalized Constants)

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_S \\ I_S \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_R \\ I_R \end{bmatrix}$$

For a long line of length $l$:

$$A = D = \cosh(\gamma l)$$

$$B = Z_C \cdot \sinh(\gamma l)$$

$$C = \frac{1}{Z_C} \cdot \sinh(\gamma l)$$

For a **lossless** long line ($\alpha = 0$, so $\gamma l = j\beta l$):

$$A = D = \cos(\beta l)$$

$$B = jZ_C \sin(\beta l)$$

$$C = j\frac{\sin(\beta l)}{Z_C}$$

### 2.3.5 Equivalent-π Model of a Long Line
The rigorous distributed model can be represented as a **lumped π-circuit** with modified parameters:

$$Z'_{eq} = Z_C \cdot \sinh(\gamma l)$$

$$\frac{Y'_{eq}}{2} = \frac{\tanh(\gamma l / 2)}{Z_C}$$

> [!TIP]
> The equivalent-π of a long line looks identical in shape to the nominal-π, but the values of $Z$ and $Y/2$ are **not** the total $z \cdot l$ and $y \cdot l$ — they are **corrected using hyperbolic functions**.

## 2.4 Special Engineering Phenomena

### 2.4.1 Ferranti Effect
When a long line is **lightly loaded or open-circuited at the receiving end**, the receiving-end voltage becomes **greater than the sending-end voltage**.

$$\frac{V_R}{V_S} = \frac{1}{\cos(\beta l)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a 400 kV, 400 km line in Kerala (e.g., Edamon–Trivandrum stretch), Ferranti effect can raise $V_R$ by **5–8%** above $V_S$ if the receiving end is open. This is why KSEB uses **shunt reactors** at substations.

### 2.4.2 Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)

$$\text{SIL} = \frac{|V|^2}{Z_C}$$

This is the natural loading at which the line consumes zero net reactive power. For a 400 kV line with $Z_C = 400\ \Omega$:

$$\text{SIL} = \frac{(400{,}000)^2}{400} = 400\ \text{MW}$$

### 2.4.3 Voltage Regulation (Long Line)

$$\%\text{Reg} = \frac{|V_S|/|A| - |V_R|}{|V_R|} \times 100$$

### 2.4.4 Transmission Efficiency

$$\%\eta = \frac{|V_R| \cdot |I_R| \cdot \cos\phi_R}{|V_S| \cdot |I_S| \cdot \cos\phi_S} \times 100$$

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Series impedance (short line) | $Z = R + jX_L$ | $R, X_L$ in Ω |
| 2 | Short-line voltage drop | $V_S = V_R + IZ$ | Phasor form |
| 3 | Approx. regulation (short) | $\frac{IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi}{V_R}\times 100$ | Per unit |
| 4 | Nominal-π $V_S$ | $V_S = V_R + I_R Z + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} Z$ | Approx form |
| 5 | Nominal-π $I_S$ | $I_S = I_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + V_R Y$ | Exact form |
| 6 | Nominal-T $V_S$ | $V_S = V_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R Z$ | Exact form |
| 7 | Nominal-T $I_S$ | $I_S = I_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) + V_R Y$ | Approx form |
| 8 | Surge impedance | $Z_C = \sqrt{z/y}$ | Ω, real for lossless |
| 9 | Propagation constant | $\gamma = \sqrt{zy} = \alpha + j\beta$ | per km |
| 10 | Wavelength | $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta$ | km |
| 11 | ABCD constants (long) | $A=D=\cosh\gamma l$, $B=Z_C\sinh\gamma l$, $C=\sinh\gamma l/Z_C$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | ABCD lossless | $A=\cos\beta l$, $B=jZ_C\sin\beta l$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Equiv-π Z' | $Z_C \sinh(\gamma l)$ | Ω |
| 14 | Equiv-π Y'/2 | $\tanh(\gamma l/2)/Z_C$ | S |
| 15 | SIL | $V^2 / Z_C$ | MW |
| 16 | Ferranti ratio | $1/\cos(\beta l)$ | Per unit |
| 17 | Power transfer (lossless) | $P = \frac{V_S V_R}{Z_C}\sin\delta$ | δ = load angle |
| 18 | Line efficiency | $\frac{P_R}{P_R + 3I^2R} \times 100$ | Percentage |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Out of the 14-mark Part-B question, 7 marks are usually for a **numerical problem** (e.g., "A 200 km, 3-phase, 50 Hz transmission line has...") and 7 marks for a **derivation** (e.g., "Derive ABCD parameters of a long line"). Practice both formats.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Short-Line Voltage Regulation (Exact)

### Given:
- $V_R$ at receiving end (reference phasor)
- Load current $I$ lagging $V_R$ by $\phi$
- Series impedance $Z = R + jX_L$

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Phasor representation of the load current**

$$I = I\angle{-\phi_R} = I(\cos\phi_R - j\sin\phi_R)$$

**Step 2 — Impedance drop phasor**

$$IZ = I\angle{-\phi_R} \cdot (R + jX_L)$$

Expanding:

$$IZ = I(R\cos\phi_R + X_L\sin\phi_R) + jI(X_L\cos\phi_R - R\sin\phi_R)$$

**Step 3 — Sending-end voltage as a phasor sum**

$$V_S = V_R + IZ$$

$$V_S = \big[V_R + I(R\cos\phi_R + X_L\sin\phi_R)\big] + j\big[I(X_L\cos\phi_R - R\sin\phi_R)\big]$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of $V_S$**

$$\vert V_S \vert = \sqrt{\big[V_R + I(R\cos\phi_R + X_L\sin\phi_R)\big]^2 + \big[I(X_L\cos\phi_R - R\sin\phi_R)\big]^2}$$

**Step 5 — Exact voltage regulation**

$$\%\text{Reg} = \frac{\vert V_S \vert - V_R}{V_R} \times 100$$

**Step 6 — Approximation for small drops (used in 90% of KTU problems)**

Since $IR \ll V_R$ and $IX_L \ll V_R$, we can approximate:

$$\vert V_S \vert \approx V_R + I(R\cos\phi_R + X_L\sin\phi_R)$$

Thus:

$$\%\text{Reg} \approx \frac{I(R\cos\phi_R + X_L\sin\phi_R)}{V_R} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term $(R\cos\phi_R + X_L\sin\phi_R)$ is called the **effective resistance** of the line. For purely inductive lines, $R = 0$ and regulation $= IX_L\sin\phi_R / V_R$. For unity power factor, $\cos\phi_R = 1$ and only $IR$ contributes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ABCD Parameters from Telegraphist's Equations

### Step 1 — Write the two first-order ODEs

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = z \cdot I \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\frac{dI}{dx} = y \cdot V \quad \text{...(2)}$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate (1) with respect to $x$

$$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = z \cdot \frac{dI}{dx}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute (2) into the above

$$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = z \cdot y \cdot V = \gamma^2 V$$

where $\gamma^2 = zy$.

### Step 4 — General solution of the second-order ODE

The characteristic equation is $m^2 - \gamma^2 = 0$, giving $m = \pm\gamma$.

$$V(x) = K_1 e^{\gamma x} + K_2 e^{-\gamma x}$$

### Step 5 — Find $I(x)$ by differentiating and using (1)

$$I(x) = \frac{1}{z}\frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{\gamma}{z}(K_1 e^{\gamma x} - K_2 e^{-\gamma x})$$

### Step 6 — Define characteristic impedance

$$Z_C = \frac{z}{\gamma} = \sqrt{\frac{z}{y}}$$

So:

$$I(x) = \frac{1}{Z_C}(K_1 e^{\gamma x} - K_2 e^{-\gamma x})$$

### Step 7 — Apply boundary conditions at $x = 0$ (receiving end)

At $x = 0$:

$$V(0) = V_R = K_1 + K_2$$

$$I(0) = I_R = \frac{1}{Z_C}(K_1 - K_2)$$

Solving:

$$K_1 = \frac{V_R + Z_C I_R}{2}$$

$$K_2 = \frac{V_R - Z_C I_R}{2}$$

### Step 8 — Apply at sending end ($x = l$)

Substitute $x = l$ into the general solution:

$$V_S = K_1 e^{\gamma l} + K_2 e^{-\gamma l}$$

$$I_S = \frac{1}{Z_C}(K_1 e^{\gamma l} - K_2 e^{-\gamma l})$$

### Step 9 — Express in hyperbolic form

Using $e^{\gamma l} = \cosh(\gamma l) + \sinh(\gamma l)$ and $e^{-\gamma l} = \cosh(\gamma l) - \sinh(\gamma l)$:

$$V_S = \cosh(\gamma l) \cdot V_R + Z_C \sinh(\gamma l) \cdot I_R$$

$$I_S = \frac{1}{Z_C}\sinh(\gamma l) \cdot V_R + \cosh(\gamma l) \cdot I_R$$

### Step 10 — Identify the ABCD constants

$$A = \cosh(\gamma l), \quad B = Z_C \sinh(\gamma l), \quad C = \frac{\sinh(\gamma l)}{Z_C}, \quad D = \cosh(\gamma l)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $A = D$ for a *symmetrical* line, and $AD - BC = 1$ is the **reciprocity condition** (also checkable using $\cosh^2 - \sinh^2 = 1$).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent-π of a Long Line

### Step 1 — Start with the ABCD sending-end equations

$$V_S = A V_R + B I_R$$

$$I_S = C V_R + A I_R$$

### Step 2 — Express $I_R$ from the second equation in terms of $I_S$ and $V_R$

$$I_R = A I_S - C V_R$$

Substitute into the first:

$$V_S = A V_R + B(A I_S - C V_R) = (A - BC/A) V_R + AB \cdot I_S$$

### Step 3 — Recognition of equivalent-π topology

For a π-network, the equations are:

$$V_S = V_R + Z'_{eq} I_{line}$$

where $I_{line} = I_R + (Y'/2) V_R$ at the receiving-end capacitor side.

By algebraic manipulation, the equivalent series impedance is:

$$Z'_{eq} = B = Z_C \sinh(\gamma l)$$

And the equivalent half-shunt admittance is:

$$\frac{Y'_{eq}}{2} = \frac{A - 1}{B} = \frac{\cosh(\gamma l) - 1}{Z_C \sinh(\gamma l)}$$

### Step 4 — Hyperbolic simplification using half-angle identity

Using $\cosh\theta - 1 = 2\sinh^2(\theta/2)$ and $\sinh\theta = 2\sinh(\theta/2)\cosh(\theta/2)$:

$$\frac{Y'_{eq}}{2} = \frac{2\sinh^2(\gamma l/2)}{Z_C \cdot 2\sinh(\gamma l/2)\cosh(\gamma l/2)} = \frac{\tanh(\gamma l/2)}{Z_C}$$

### Step 5 — Final equivalent-π result

$$\boxed{Z'_{eq} = Z_C \sinh(\gamma l), \qquad \frac{Y'_{eq}}{2} = \frac{\tanh(\gamma l/2)}{Z_C}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Nominal-π Performance (Exact ABCD)

### Step 1 — Define total line constants
$Z = R + jX_L$ (total series) and $Y = G + jB_C$ (total shunt).

### Step 2 — Write the exact ABCD matrix for nominal-π

For a π-network with series $Z$ in the middle and shunt $Y/2$ at each end, cascade analysis gives:

$$A = D = 1 + \frac{ZY}{2}$$

$$B = Z$$

$$C = Y\left(1 + \frac{ZY}{4}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Verification that $AD - BC = 1$

$$AD - BC = \left(1 + \frac{ZY}{2}\right)^2 - ZY\left(1 + \frac{ZY}{4}\right)$$

$$= 1 + ZY + \frac{Z^2Y^2}{4} - ZY - \frac{Z^2Y^2}{4} = 1$$

> [!TIP]
> This confirms the **reciprocity property** of passive two-port networks — a critical concept tested in KTU university exams.

### Step 4 — Sending-end quantities

$$V_S = \left(1 + \frac{ZY}{2}\right)V_R + Z I_R$$

$$I_S = Y\left(1 + \frac{ZY}{4}\right)V_R + \left(1 + \frac{ZY}{2}\right)I_R$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Power Transmitted by a Lossless Long Line

### Step 1 — Use lossless ABCD

For a lossless line: $A = \cos\beta l$, $B = jZ_C\sin\beta l$.

$$V_S = V_R \cos\beta l + j I_R Z_C \sin\beta l$$

### Step 2 — Express receiving-end current with load angle

Let $V_R = V_R \angle 0$ and $V_S = V_S \angle \delta$.

$$I_R = \frac{V_S \angle \delta - V_R \cos\beta l}{jZ_C \sin\beta l}$$

### Step 3 — Complex power at receiving end

$$S_R = V_R I_R^* = \frac{V_R (V_S \angle{-\delta} - V_R \cos\beta l)}{-jZ_C \sin\beta l}$$

### Step 4 — Real power transferred

After algebraic manipulation (taking the real part):

$$\boxed{P_R = \frac{V_S V_R}{Z_C \sin\beta l}\sin\delta}$$

This is the **steady-state power-angle equation** of a lossless long line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a short line ($\beta l \to 0$), $\sin\beta l \to \beta l$ and $Z_C \sin\beta l \to X_L$, recovering the familiar $P = V_S V_R / X \cdot \sin\delta$.
