I_{a1} Z_1 = 1.0 - (-j0.3602)(j1.65) = 1.0 - 0.5943 = 0.4057 \text{ pu} $$ $$ V_{a2} = -I_{a2} Z_2 = -(j0.2459)(j1.65) = 0.4057 \text{ pu} $$ $$ V_{a0} = -I_{a0} Z_0 = -(j0.1143)(j3.55) = 0.4058 \text{ pu} $$ $$ V_a = V_{a0} + V_{a1} + V_{a2} = 0.4057 + 0.4057 + 0.4058 = 1.2172 \text{ pu} $$ So the healthy phase voltage rises to **1.217 pu** (about 22% above nominal) — a phenomenon known as **healthy phase over-voltage** in DLG faults, which is critical for insulation design and surge arrester rating. ### Question B — Module Internal Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** > A power system has two generators $G_1$ and $G_2$ connected in parallel to a bus, which feeds a transmission line. A 3-phase fault occurs at the far end of the line. > > **Data on 100 MVA base:** > - $G_1$: 50 MVA, $X_d'' = 0.2$ pu; $G_2$: 50 MVA, $X_d'' = 0.25$ pu > - Transmission line: $X = 0.4$ pu, $R = 0.1$ pu > > **(a)** Calculate the fault current and the fault MVA at the receiving end. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** If a **line-to-line fault** occurs at the same point, calculate the fault current in the faulted phases. State the assumption for negative sequence reactance of generators. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1: Convert generator reactances to common 100 MVA base:** $$ X_{G1} = 0.2 \times \frac{100}{50} = 0.4 \text{ pu} $$ $$ X_{G2} = 0.25 \times \frac{100}{50} = 0.5 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 2: Thevenin equivalent reactance at the receiving end:** Generators are in parallel at the sending bus: $$ X_{G1 \vert\vert G2} = \frac{X_{G1} \cdot X_{G2}}{X_{G1} + X_{G2}} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.5}{0.4 + 0.5} = \frac{0.20}{0.9} = 0.2222 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 3: Add line impedance to get total Thevenin impedance at fault point:** $$ Z_{th} = R_L + j(X_{G \vert\vert} + X_L) = 0.1 + j(0.2222 + 0.4) = 0.1 + j0.6222 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 4: Magnitude of Thevenin impedance:** $$ \vert Z_{th} \vert = \sqrt{0.1^2 + 0.6222^2} = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.3871} = \sqrt{0.3971} = 0.6302 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 5: Fault current in per-unit (3-phase fault):** $$ I_{f,pu} = \frac{1.0}{0.6302} = 1.587 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 6: Fault MVA:** $$ MVA_{sc} = I_{f,pu} \times S_{base} = 1.587 \times 100 = 158.7 \text{ MVA} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b): Line-to-Line Fault **Step 1: Assumption** — The negative sequence reactance of a synchronous generator is approximately equal to its subtransient reactance: $X_2 \approx X_d''$. This is a standard KTU assumption for cylindrical rotor machines. **Step 2: Negative sequence reactances on 100 MVA base:** $$ X_{2,G1} = 0.4 \text{ pu}, \quad X_{2,G2} = 0.5 \text{ pu} $$ $$ X_{2,line} = 0.4 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 3: Total negative sequence impedance to fault point:** $$ X_{2,G1 \vert\vert G2} = 0.2222 \text{ pu} $$ $$ X_{2,total} = X_{2,G \vert\vert} + X_{2,line} = 0.2222 + 0.4 = 0.6222 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 4: Positive sequence impedance (same as in 3-phase fault for the inductive part):** $$ X_{1,total} = 0.6222 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 5: For LL fault, the positive and negative sequence networks are in series (from the perspective of the source), so:** $$ I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{1.0\angle 0°}{j(0.6222 + 0.6222) + 0.1 + 0.1} = \frac{1.0}{0.2 + j1.2444} $$ **Step 6: Compute the magnitude:** $$ \vert Z_1 + Z_2 \vert = \sqrt{0.2^2 + 1.2444^2} = \sqrt{0.04 + 1.5485} = \sqrt{1.5885} = 1.2604 \text{ pu} $$ $$ \vert I_{a1} \vert = \frac{1.0}{1.2604} = 0.7934 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 7: Fault current in faulted phase b (or c):** $$ \vert I_f \vert = \sqrt{3} \cdot \vert I_{a1} \vert = 1.732 \times 0.7934 = 1.374 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 8: Conclusion:** The LL fault current is **1.374 pu**, which is **86.6%** of the 3-phase fault current (1.587 pu). This confirms the classical result that the LL fault is the **least severe** unsymmetrical fault in solidly grounded systems. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to change base MVA:** When generators have different MVA ratings than the system base, students often forget to apply the base-change formula $X_{new} = X_{old} \times (S_{new}/S_{old}) \times (V_{old}/V_{new})^2$. This causes up to **3 marks deducted** per error. > 2. **Mixing up sequence subscripts:** Using $I_{a0}$ where $I_{a1}$ is needed (or vice versa) is a classic error. The boundary conditions dictate which is which. > 3. **Wrong sequence network connection:** SLG = series, LL = parallel ($Z_1 \vert\mid Z_2$), DLG = all parallel with $Z_1$ in series with $(Z_2 \vert\mid Z_0)$. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 4-mark penalty. > 4. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor for LL fault:** The line-to-line fault current is $\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{a1}$, not just $I_{a1}$. Many students miss this. > 5. **Neglecting the inverse Fortescue transformation for DLG:** For SLG, the phase current is just $3 I_{a0}$, but for DLG you must use the full inverse matrix to get the currents in faulted phases b and c. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Faults (Shunt Only)** - [x] **Symmetrical 3-phase fault** is the most severe. Fault current $I_f = 1/X_{th}$ in pu, fault MVA $= I_f \times S_{base}$. - [x] **Symmetrical Components** are the unique decomposition of any unbalanced 3-phase system into three balanced sets: positive (abc), negative (acb), and zero (in-phase). - [x] **Fortescue operator** $a = 1\angle 120°$ satisfies $1 + a + a^2 = 0$. - [x] **Zero-sequence current** exists only if there is a return path through neutral/ground. It equals $I_{a0} = (I_a + I_b + I_c)/3$. - [x] **Negative-sequence network** has no EMF source. Generator $X_2 \approx X_d''$. - [x] **Zero-sequence network** depends on transformer connections: $\Delta$ blocks, Y-grounded allows, Y-ungrounded blocks. - [x] **SLG fault** uses all three sequence networks in **series**: $I_{a0} = I_{a1} = I_{a2} = E_a/(Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0)$, $I_f = 3 I_{a0}$. - [x] **LL fault** uses positive and negative in **parallel** (from source side): $I_{a1} = E_a/(Z_1 + Z_2)$, $I_f = \sqrt{3} \cdot \vert I_{a1} \vert$. - [x] **DLG fault** uses all three in **parallel combination**: $I_{a1} = E_a/[Z_1 + (Z_2 \vert\mid Z_0)]$. Phase currents require inverse Fortescue transformation. - [x] **Per-unit system** is essential. Base MVA is uniform across the network; base kV changes at each voltage level. $I_{base} = S_{base}/(\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{base})$. - [x] **Base change formula**: $X_{pu,new} = X_{pu,old} \times (S_{base,new}/S_{base,old}) \times (V_{base,old}/V_{base,new})^2$. - [x] **Healthy phase over-voltage** in DLG faults can exceed 1.0 pu, requiring surge arrester coordination. - [x] **Relative severity ranking** (typical): 3-phase $\geq$ SLG (solidly grounded) $\geq$ DLG $\geq$ LL. - [x] **Assumption to remember**: Generator negative-sequence reactance $X_2 = X_d''$ (subtransient). - [x] **Resistance is usually neglected** for HV fault calculations in KTU problems unless explicitly given.

topics.title: Need for Protection - Types of Protection Schemes, Primary and Back-up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT503 - POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Need for Protection - Types of Protection Schemes, Primary and Back-up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Fundamental Need for Power System Protection

A modern power system is a vast, interconnected network comprising **generators**, **transformers**, **transmission lines**, **distribution feeders**, and a multitude of switching/protective devices. Such a system is perpetually exposed to internal and external abnormalities collectively termed as **faults** (or disturbances). These include short circuits (phase-to-phase, phase-to-ground, three-phase), overloading, over-voltages, under-voltages, under-frequency, and loss of synchronism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Power System Protection is defined as the scheme, arrangement, and coordinated use of protective relays, circuit breakers, and associated apparatus designed to detect, localize, and isolate abnormal or fault conditions in a power system in the fastest possible time, while maintaining stability, continuity of service, equipment safety, and personnel protection.*

The "need" arises from the fact that fault currents can be **5 to 20 times** the normal rated current, and fault-induced energy ($I^2Rt$) dissipated in a transformer winding or bus-bar in just a few hundred milliseconds can cause **permanent thermal and mechanical destruction**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Nervous System**
> Imagine the human body. When you touch a hot surface, the **sensory nerves (Primary Protection)** instantly signal the **spinal cord (Relay)**, which commands the hand (Circuit Breaker) to withdraw in milliseconds. Simultaneously, the **brain's pain memory (Back-up Protection)** ensures that if the spinal reflex somehow fails, the conscious mind will still pull the hand away.
> In the same way, the **Current/Voltage Transformers (Sensors) → Protective Relays (Decision-makers) → Circuit Breakers (Muscles)** work in unison, while a **back-up relay** stands ready in case the primary relay mis-operates.

### 1.3 Primary Objectives of Protection (The Five Pillars)

| # | Objective | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Speed** | Operate typically within **1 to 3 cycles** (20–60 ms) for high-voltage systems. |
| 2 | **Selectivity (Discrimination)** | Isolate **only** the faulty section, leaving the healthy part energized. |
| 3 | **Sensitivity** | Reliably detect even the smallest fault within the protected zone. |
| 4 | **Reliability** | **Dependability** (operates when required) + **Security** (does not operate falsely). |
| 5 | **Economy** | Provides adequate protection at a reasonable cost. |

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in Protection

- **Fault Clearing Time**: Time from fault inception to arc extinction. Standard benchmark ≤ **80 ms** for **400 kV** and above.
- **Relay Operating Time**: Usually **10–40 ms** for static/numerical relays.
- **Breaker Interrupting Time**: Typically **2 to 3 cycles** (**33–50 ms** for a 50 Hz system).
- **Discrimination Time**: The intentional time delay introduced between primary and back-up relays, usually **0.2 to 0.5 seconds**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Faults Necessitating Protection

The protection scheme must respond to the following principal fault categories:

1. **Symmetrical Faults** — Three-phase short circuits (`L-L-L` and `L-L-L-G`). These are the **most severe (≈ 10–20 p.u. current)** but rarest (≈ 5% occurrence).
2. **Unsymmetrical Faults** — `L-G` (≈ 70% occurrence), `L-L` (≈ 15%), `L-L-G` (≈ 10%). These are more frequent but produce **lower fault levels**.
3. **Abnormal Operating Conditions** — Overload, over-voltage, under-voltage, under-frequency, loss of excitation, and **power swing**.

### 2.2 Types of Protection Schemes (Unit vs. Non-Unit)

Protection schemes are broadly classified into two engineering families:

#### 2.2.1 Non-Unit (Zone) Protection
The relay operates based on **local measurements** (current, voltage, impedance) at its own location. **There are no explicit zone boundaries**; the protection extends indefinitely along the line.

- **Overcurrent Protection**
- **Distance (Impedance) Protection**
- **Directional Overcurrent Protection**
- **Earth-Fault Protection**

#### 2.2.2 Unit Protection
The relay operates **only for faults within a precisely defined zone**, using measurements at **both ends** of the protected element. It is inherently **discriminative** and **instantaneous**.

- **Differential Protection** (Merz-Price, Biased Differential)
- **Pilot-Wire Protection**
- **Distance Protection with Telepilot (Permissive/PUTT schemes)**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|----------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_{pre-fault}}{Z_{th}}$ | Symmetrical short-circuit current magnitude | Amperes (A) |
| 2 | $\%Z = \dfrac{Z_{pu} \times V_{Base}^2}{S_{Base}}$ | Percentage impedance of transformer/line | % |
| 3 | $Z_{pu} = \dfrac{Z_{actual} \cdot S_{Base}}{V_{Base}^2}$ | Per-unit impedance conversion | p.u. |
| 4 | $MVA_{sc} = \dfrac{V_{LL}^2}{\%Z \cdot V_{Base}^2} \cdot S_{Base}$ | Fault MVA at any point | MVA |
| 5 | $PSM = \dfrac{I_{fault,min}}{I_{pickup}}$ | Plug Setting Multiplier (Overcurrent relay) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $t = TMS \cdot \beta \cdot \left[ \dfrac{A}{PSM^{\alpha}-1} + L \right]$ | Standard Inverse IDMT characteristic (IEC 60255-151) | seconds |
| 7 | $Z_{reach} = k \cdot V_{rated} \big/ I_{rated}$ | Reach setting of distance relay (primary ohms) | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $I_{d} = I_{1} + I_{2}$ (ideal case) | Differential current (operate quantity) | A |
| 9 | $I_{r} = \dfrac{I_{1} - I_{2}}{2}$ | Restraining current (bias quantity) | A |
| 10 | $K = \dfrac{I_{d}}{I_{r}} \cdot 100\%$ | Percentage bias slope (typically 20%–40%) | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard IDMT constants (IEC 60255-151)**: Standard Inverse → $A = 0.14$, $\alpha = 0.02$, $\beta = 0.0515$, $L = 0$. Very Inverse → $A = 13.5$, $\alpha = 1.0$. Extremely Inverse → $A = 80$, $\alpha = 2.0$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These protection schemes safeguard assets such as:
- **Step-up transformers** at generating stations (Differential + REF + Overcurrent).
- **EHV transmission lines** (Distance Protection Scheme — Zone 1, 2, 3).
- **Generator stator windings** (Differential + Stator Earth-fault + Loss-of-excitation).
- **HVDC converter stations** (DC differential, valve-side overcurrent, commutation failure detection).

The **selection of scheme** is dictated by voltage level, fault level, criticality, cost, and operating speed. For example, distance protection is preferred above **66 kV** while overcurrent suffices below **33 kV**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Fault MVA at a Bus (Analytical Core)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU University Exams (12-mark problems)**. Mastering the algebraic transition steps is mandatory.

**Problem Setup:** A generator delivers rated voltage $V$ through a transformer of percentage impedance $\%Z$. Determine the fault level (in MVA) when a three-phase short circuit occurs at the secondary of the transformer.

**Step 1 — Identify the base values on the fault side of the transformer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{Base} &= S_{rated} \text{ of transformer (MVA)} \\
V_{Base} &= V_{secondary,LL} \text{ of transformer (kV)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert percentage impedance to per-unit form:**

$$
Z_{pu} = \dfrac{\%Z}{100}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the symmetric short-circuit current formula:**

The pre-fault voltage is taken as $1.0 \text{ p.u.}$ (rated). The Thévenin impedance seen from the fault point is $Z_{pu}$. Hence, fault current in per-unit is:

$$
I_{f,pu} = \dfrac{V_{pre-fault}}{Z_{pu}} = \dfrac{1.0}{Z_{pu}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert per-unit current to actual fault current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{base} &= \dfrac{S_{Base}}{\sqrt{3}\cdot V_{Base}} \\
I_{f,actual} &= I_{f,pu} \cdot I_{base} = \dfrac{I_{base}}{Z_{pu}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert fault current to fault MVA (the engineering metric used for switchgear rating):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MVA_{sc} &= \sqrt{3}\cdot V_{Base} \cdot I_{f,actual} \times 10^{-3} \\
&= \sqrt{3}\cdot V_{Base} \cdot \dfrac{I_{base}}{Z_{pu}} \times 10^{-3} \\
&= \sqrt{3}\cdot V_{Base} \cdot \dfrac{S_{Base}/(\sqrt{3}\cdot V_{Base})}{Z_{pu}} \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final simplified expression:**

$$
\boxed{MVA_{sc} = \dfrac{S_{Base}}{Z_{pu}} = \dfrac{100 \cdot S_{Base}}{\%Z}}
$$

This is the **fundamental switchgear-rating equation** used by every utility engineer.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Protection Schemes (KTU High-Yield)

| Protection Scheme | Principle of Operation | Operating Speed | Primary Use | Limitation |
|------------------|------------------------|----------------|-------------|------------|
| **Overcurrent (IDMT)** | Current exceeds pickup setting; inverse-time delay | 0.1 – 3.0 s | Radial feeders, transformers < 10 MVA | Cannot discriminate in ring/mesh networks |
| **Differential (Merz-Price)** | Compare currents entering and leaving the zone | < 30 ms (instantaneous) | Transformers, generators, busbars, motors | Needs CTs at both ends; CT saturation risk |
| **Distance (Impedance)** | Measures $V/I$ apparent impedance along the line | 1 – 3 cycles | EHV/UHV transmission lines | Insecure for high-resistance earth faults |
| **Directional Overcurrent** | Current exceeding pickup **with a specific phase angle** | 0.1 – 1.0 s | Parallel feeders, ring mains | Needs voltage-polarising quantity |
| **Pilot-Wire** | Compares currents at line ends via metallic pilot cable | < 50 ms | Short HV cables (< 20 km) | Expensive; susceptible to pilot-cable faults |
| **Earth-Fault (Zero-Sequence)** | Detects residual / zero-sequence current $3I_0$ | 0.1 – 1.0 s | Ungrounded/resonant-grounded systems | Ineffective for phase-to-phase faults |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Discrimination (Time-Grading) Logic

In a radial feeder, the relay nearest the source sees the **highest fault current** but must have the **longest time delay**; the relay nearest the load sees **lower current** but acts **first**. This is achieved through the IEC IDMT curve.

**Step 1 — Compute the Plug Setting Multiplier:**

$$
PSM = \dfrac{I_{fault,primary}}{CT_{ratio} \cdot I_{pickup}}
$$

**Step 2 — Read the operating time from the standard inverse curve OR compute it analytically:**

$$
t = TMS \cdot \beta \cdot \left[ \dfrac{A}{PSM^{\alpha}-1} + L \right]
$$

**Step 3 — For Standard Inverse ($\alpha = 0.02$, $A = 0.14$, $\beta = 0.0515$, $L = 0$):**

$$
t = TMS \cdot 0.0515 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{0.14}{PSM^{0.02}-1} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Grading margin:**
- Discriminative time interval $\Delta t = 0.4 \text{ s}$ (typical).
- Relay R2 (farther from load) → $t_{R2} = t_{R1} + \Delta t$.
- Continue upstream to R3, R4, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> This grading logic is the foundation of **discrimination by time**, **discrimination by current**, and **discrimination by both time and current** — all direct KTU 2024 syllabus topics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture: Generic Power-System Protection Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Bus1[Power System Element] --> CT[Current Transformer CT]
    Bus1 --> VT[Voltage Transformer VT]
    CT --> Relay[Protective Relay Logic]
    VT --> Relay
    Relay -->|Trip Command| Breaker[Circuit Breaker]
    Breaker -->|Isolate| Bus1
    Relay -.->|Indication, Annunciation| Panel[SCADA / Annunciator Panel]
    Relay -.->|Event Log| Logger[Fault Recorder]
    Back1[Back-up Relay] -.-> Breaker
    Back1 -.-> Relay
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Primary vs Back-up Protection Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F[Inception of Fault on Transmission Line] --> P1[Zone 1 Primary Distance Relay: 80 percent reach]
    F --> P2[Zone 2 Primary Distance Relay: 120 percent reach - 400 ms]
    F --> P3[Zone 3 Primary Distance Relay: Next bus reach - 800 ms]
    F --> BU1[Remote Back-up: Adjacent line distance relay]
    F --> BU2[Local Back-up: Duplicate relay on same bay]
    P1 -->|Trip 20 ms| CB1[Local Circuit Breaker]
    BU1 -->|Trip if primary fails| CB2[Adjacent Line Breakers]
    BU2 -->|Trip if primary relay fails| CB1
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Discrimination/Coordination Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Power System Energised]) --> Monitor[Continuous Monitoring of I, V, Z, f]
    Monitor --> Detect{Fault Detected?}
    Detect -->|No| Monitor
    Detect -->|Yes Inside Zone| Pri[Primary Relay Operates]
    Detect -->|Yes Inside Zone| Back[Back-up Relay Waits for Discriminating Time]
    Pri --> CBtrip[Trip Local Breaker - 60 to 100 ms]
    CBtrip --> Isolate[Fault Isolated]
    Isolate --> Restore[Auto Reclosing OR Manual Restoration]
    Back -->|Primary Operated within Delta t| Reset[Back-up Resets - No Trip]
    Back -->|Primary Fails beyond Delta t| BackCB[Trip Back-up Breakers]
    BackCB --> Isolate
    Restore --> Monitor
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture: Differential Protection (Merz-Price Scheme)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Incoming Winding End] -->|CT1| RL1[Relay Coil End 1]
    W2[Outgoing Winding End] -->|CT2| RL2[Relay Coil End 2]
    RL1 --> RL3[Operating Coil - Operate Quantity]
    RL2 --> RL4[Restraining Coil - Bias Quantity]
    RL3 --> Trip[If I_op > K x I_res, Trip Command]
    RL4 --> Trip
    Trip --> CB[Circuit Breaker Trip Coil]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three essential qualities/requirements of a protective relay.*

**Model Answer:**
A protective relay must possess the following qualities:
1. **Selectivity (Discrimination):** It must isolate only the faulty section, leaving the healthy part in service.
2. **Sensitivity:** It must reliably detect the smallest fault current within its protected zone.
3. **Reliability:** It must be **dependable** (operate when required) and **secure** (not operate falsely).
*(Additionally, students may quote speed, simplicity, and economy for full credit.)* — **3 marks**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Unit and Non-Unit protection schemes with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Unit Protection | Non-Unit Protection |
|---------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Zone | Clearly defined, finite | Undefined, extends along line |
| Principle | Comparison of currents at both ends | Local measurement of current/voltage/impedance |
| Speed | Instantaneous (< 30 ms) | Time-delayed (0.1 – 3 s) |
| Example | **Differential (Merz-Price)** | **Overcurrent (IDMT)** | — **3 marks**

---

### Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-3 Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **need for power system protection**, and list the **essential qualities of a protective relay**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **classification of protection schemes** into unit and non-unit types, with suitable examples and a comparative diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Need for Power System Protection (7 marks)**

Power systems are subject to both **internal faults** (insulation failure, ageing, overloading) and **external abnormalities** (lightning surges, switching transients, falling trees on lines). The principal reasons protection is indispensable are:

1. **Personnel Safety:** Faults can produce arcing, fire, and step/touch potentials lethal to humans.
2. **Equipment Protection:** A short circuit can deliver 5–20 p.u. current, dissipating $I^2Rt$ energy in milliseconds and causing irreversible thermal/mechanical damage to windings, bushings, and cables.
3. **System Stability:** Uncontrolled faults lead to loss of synchronism and cascading blackouts (e.g., the **2003 North-East Blackout** affected 55 million people).
4. **Continuity of Supply:** Selective isolation keeps the rest of the grid operational.
5. **Economic Loss Minimisation:** Reduces repair costs and revenue loss.

**Essential Qualities of a Protective Relay:**
- **Selectivity, Speed, Sensitivity, Reliability (Dependability + Security), Simplicity, Economy.** (Write any 5 in brief with one-line definitions for full credit.)

**(b) Classification of Protection Schemes (7 marks)**

| Non-Unit (Zone) Protection | Unit Protection |
|----------------------------|-----------------|
| Operates from local measurements | Operates from measurements at both ends |
| Zone of protection is **not sharply defined** | Zone of protection is **sharply defined** |
| Requires time/current grading for discrimination | Inherently **selective and instantaneous** |
| Examples: Overcurrent, Distance, Directional | Examples: Differential, Pilot-Wire |

A radial feeder with three overcurrent relays (R1, R2, R3) and a Merz-Price differential scheme for the transformer can be drawn to illustrate the concept. **Merits of unit protection**: high speed, no grading required. **Demerits**: expensive, requires duplicate CTs and pilot cables.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Define **Primary and Back-up Protection**. With a neat block diagram, explain the **two types of back-up protection** (Local and Remote). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **10 MVA, 11 kV/66 kV** transformer has a percentage impedance of **8%**. Calculate the **fault MVA and fault current** on the HT side for a three-phase short circuit at the LT terminals. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Primary and Back-up Protection (7 marks)**

- **Primary (or Main) Protection:** The first line of defence intended to operate **instantaneously or with minimum intentional delay** for any fault within its assigned zone. Failure of primary protection should not endanger the system.
- **Back-up Protection:** The second line of defence, designed to operate **only if the primary protection fails to clear the fault** within a predetermined discriminating time interval.

**Types of Back-up Protection:**

1. **Remote Back-up:** The back-up relay is installed in an **adjacent substation** (typically 1–2 bays away). It monitors the same line and trips its own breaker if the primary relay/breaker of the faulted line fails. *Advantage:* simple, low cost, no duplication. *Disadvantage:* disconnects a larger healthy area.

2. **Local Back-up:** A **duplicate relay (and often a duplicate trip coil on the breaker)** is installed in the **same bay** as the primary. It operates the same breaker if the primary relay fails. *Advantage:* fault is cleared by the local breaker only — minimum disturbance. *Disadvantage:* expensive (duplicate hardware).

*(Include a single-line diagram showing Primary Relay + Remote Back-up in adjacent substation + Local Back-up duplicate relay in same bay.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $S_{Base} = 10 \text{ MVA}$, $V_{HT} = 66 \text{ kV}$, $\%Z = 8\%$.

**Step 1 — Base current on HT side:**

$$
I_{base,HT} = \dfrac{S_{Base}}{\sqrt{3}\cdot V_{HT}} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3}\times 66 \times 10^3} = 87.47 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-unit impedance:**

$$
Z_{pu} = \dfrac{8}{100} = 0.08 \text{ p.u.}
$$

**Step 3 — Fault current (in p.u.):**

$$
I_{f,pu} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.08} = 12.5 \text{ p.u.}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual fault current on HT side:**

$$
I_{f,actual} = 12.5 \times 87.47 = 1093.4 \text{ A} \approx 1.093 \text{ kA}
$$

**Step 5 — Fault MVA:**

$$
\boxed{MVA_{sc} = \dfrac{S_{Base}}{Z_{pu}} = \dfrac{10}{0.08} = 125 \text{ MVA}}
$$

**Verification:** $MVA_{sc} = \sqrt{3}\cdot V_{HT}\cdot I_{f,actual}\times 10^{-3} = \sqrt{3}\times 66 \times 1.093 = 125 \text{ MVA}$ ✔ — **7 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Many students write $V_{HT} = 66{,}000$ V but forget to convert A to kA when computing MVA. Always verify the dimensional consistency.
> 2. **Confusing LT and HT sides:** If the question specifies "fault on the LT side," use the LT voltage; the current transforms by the turns ratio automatically when using MVA (which is invariant).
> 3. **Missing the 0.866 factor:** Resistive fault components reduce fault current; only $X$ is used in the simplified $V/X$ formula assuming $R \ll X$.
> 4. **In Q.A, students often omit the diagram** of primary/back-up arrangement — a KTU 2024 scheme explicit demand. Always include a labeled single-line/block diagram.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Protection is needed for:** personnel safety, equipment survival, system stability, supply continuity, and economic reasons.
- **Five qualities of a relay:** Selectivity, Speed, Sensitivity, Reliability, Economy (mnemonic: **3 S + R + E**).
- **Fault statistics to memorise:** L-G ≈ **70%**, L-L ≈ **15%**, L-L-G ≈ **10%**, L-L-L/L-L-L-G ≈ **5%**.
- **Unit protection** is **inherently selective and instantaneous**; **non-unit protection** needs **time/current grading**.
- **Primary protection** = first to act; **Back-up protection** = second line, with $\Delta t \approx 0.4 \text{ s}$ discrimination.
- **Back-up has two types:** **Remote (adjacent substation)** and **Local (duplicate relay in same bay)**.
- **Key formula:** $MVA_{sc} = \dfrac{S_{Base}}{Z_{pu}} = \dfrac{100 \cdot S_{Base}}{\%Z}$ — must be by-heart for KTU exams.
- **IDMT Standard Inverse constants:** $A = 0.14$, $\alpha = 0.02$, $\beta = 0.0515$, $L = 0$.
- **Typical operating speeds:** Distance relay ≈ **20–40 ms**, Differential relay ≈ **< 30 ms**, IDMT overcurrent ≈ **0.1–3 s** (graded).
- **Switchgear rating equation:** $\text{Short-circuit MVA} = \sqrt{3} V_{LL} I_{sc} \times 10^{-6}$ (with $V$ in kV, $I$ in A).
- **Discrimination strategies:** *Time grading*, *Current grading*, *Time–current (dual) grading*, *Logic discrimination*, *Unit protection*.
- **Always draw a labeled diagram** in 7-mark and 14-mark questions — it is **mandatory** in the KTU 2024 scheme valuation rubric.
- **Modern trend:** Numerical (microprocessor-based) relays integrate all the above functions (overcurrent + distance + differential) into a single IED (Intelligent Electronic Device), compliant with **IEC 61850** communication protocol.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protective Relays - Basics of Typical Electromechanical Relay (Induction Type Only), Static Relays (Block Diagrams of Over Current and Instantaneous Over Current Relays), Microprocessor Relays (Block Diagram and Flow Chart of Overcurrent Relay), Fundamentals of Numerical Relays, Principles of Overcurrent, Directional, Distance and Differential Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Protective Relay — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **protective relay** is an automatic device that detects abnormal conditions (faults) in an electrical power system by continuously monitoring one or more of its operating quantities (current, voltage, frequency, impedance, phase angle, etc.), compares the monitored quantity against a preset threshold, and initiates a trip command to the circuit breaker to isolate the faulty section, while remaining stable under all normal and permissible transient conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> A relay is a device that, when activated by a specific input quantity, performs a designed function, generally by closing or opening electrical contacts, after a predetermined time delay. The relay is the brain of the protection scheme — without it, the circuit breaker is blind.

### 1.1.1 The Four Functional Pillars of Any Relay (Regardless of Technology)

| # | Function | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Sensing / Measurement** | CTs/PTs step down high currents/voltages to relay-level quantities |
| 2 | **Comparison / Discrimination** | Measured quantity is compared to a set threshold (pickup value) |
| 3 | **Decision / Logic** | Trip decision is generated if threshold is crossed |
| 4 | **Actuation** | Trip coil of circuit breaker is energised to open contacts |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Body's Nervous System

Think of a power system as a **human body** and protective relays as the **nervous system + reflexes**:

- **CTs/PTs** are the *sensory nerves* (they sense pain/pressure in fingers).
- **The relay** is the *spinal cord and brain* — it decides whether the sensation is harmful.
- **The circuit breaker** is the *muscle that pulls the hand away* (action).
- **The power system** is the body itself.

If the relay is sluggish or wrongly designed (a sluggish nerve), the body gets burned. Hence, speed, selectivity, sensitivity, and reliability are the four virtues of a good relay.

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important constant** in relay engineering is the **pickup current (Ip)**. It is the minimum value of the actuating quantity at which the relay just operates. All time-current characteristics, all discrimination, all coordination start from this value.

## 1.3 Classification of Relays (As Per KTU 2024 Module 3)

```
Relays (KTU 2024 Classification)
├── 1. Electromechanical Relays
│     ├── Electromagnetic Attracted Armature Type
│     ├── Induction Type  ← (KTU Focus)
│     │     ├── Induction Disc
│     │     └── Induction Cup
│     ├── Thermal Relays
│     └── Motor-operated Relays
├── 2. Static Relays  ← (KTU Focus)
│     ├── Analogue Comparator Based
│     └── Digital Logic Based
├── 3. Microprocessor-Based Relays  ← (KTU Focus)
└── 4. Numerical / Digital Relays  ← (KTU Focus)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare electromagnetic and static relays", always tabulate. Never write paragraphs. The 7-mark question is purely tabular + 2-line justification.

## 1.4 The Six Operating Characteristics (Functional Targets)

A relay is judged on six properties. Memorise these acronyms — they appear in **every KTU question paper**:

- **Reliability** — operates when required, does not operate when not required.
- **Selectivity (Discrimination)** — trips only the nearest upstream breaker.
- **Speed** — operates in 10–100 ms typically; never intentionally slow.
- **Sensitivity** — detects even smallest faults within its zone.
- **Stability** — does not maloperate for external faults or transients.
- **Economy** — must justify its cost in the protection scheme.

## 1.5 Basic Relay Terminology (KTU Must-Know)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Pickup Value (Ip)** | Smallest input that causes the relay to operate |
| **Reset (Dropout) Value** | Value below which the relay returns to its original state |
| **Operating Time** | Time between fault inception and contact closing |
| **Reset Time** | Time to return to normal after fault clearance |
| **Reach** | Distance (in km or %) covered by a distance relay |
| **Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)** | $\text{PSM} = \dfrac{\text{Fault Current}}{\text{Plug Setting}}$ |
| **Time Multiplier Setting (TMS)** | Adjusts the operating time of IDMT relay |
| **Burden** | Impedance imposed on CT/PT secondary by the relay |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Induction-Type Electromechanical Relay (KTU Core Topic)

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle — Physics Behind It

The induction relay works on the **same principle as a single-phase induction motor** — the interaction of two alternating fluxes, displaced in time and space, produces a **torque** on a movable aluminium disc or cup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "induction"?** Because torque is produced by *induced eddy currents* in a closed conducting surface (aluminium disc or cup). No physical connection between the magnetic circuit and the rotor is needed — just like a squirrel-cage induction motor.

### 2.1.2 Construction of an Induction Disc Relay

```
       Upper Magnet (Voltage / Current Operated)
       ┌──────┐
       │      │
   ┌───┴──┐   │
   │      │   │   Aluminium Disc (Rotor)
   │  Pole│   │   ╭───────────╮
   │  1   │   │   │           │  ← rotates in horizontal plane
   └──┬───┘   │   │   DISC    │
      │       │   ╰───────────╯
      │       │         │  
   ┌──┴───┐   │     Spiral Spring (Controlling Torque)
   │      │   │         │
   │  Pole│   │     Pivot
   │  2   │   │
   └──┬───┘   │
      │       │
   ┌──┴──┐    │
   │     │    │
   └─────┘    │
       Lower Magnet (Current Operated)
       ┌──────┐
```

### 2.1.3 Torque Production — The Mathematical Engine

Let two alternating fluxes be:
- $\phi_1$ produced by one pair of poles (proportional to current in coil 1)
- $\phi_2$ produced by the other pair of poles (proportional to current in coil 2)

These fluxes are **displaced in space by an angle $\theta$** and **displaced in time by an angle $\alpha$** (the phase angle between the two operating currents).

The instantaneous operating torque is:

$$T(t) = k \cdot \phi_1(t) \cdot \phi_2(t) \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

Averaging over one full cycle, the **average operating torque** becomes:

$$T_{op} = K \cdot \phi_1 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

where:
- $K$ = constant of proportionality (depends on geometry and frequency)
- $\phi_1, \phi_2$ = RMS values of the two alternating fluxes
- $\theta$ = spatial angle between pole axes (fixed by design, often $90°$ for maximum torque)
- $\alpha$ = phase angle between the two operating currents/fluxes in time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum torque condition:** When $\theta = 90°$ (most common design) and $\sin(\alpha) = 1$ (i.e., $\alpha = 90°$), the relay produces its maximum torque. KTU examiners often ask: *"For what condition is the torque maximum?"* — Answer: "When the two fluxes are in space quadrature and the operating currents are in time quadrature."

### 2.1.4 The Controlling (Restraining) Torque

Provided by a spiral spring:

$$T_c = K_s \cdot \theta_m$$

where:
- $K_s$ = spring constant (N·m/rad)
- $\theta_m$ = angular displacement of the disc (radians)

### 2.1.5 Operating Condition

The relay operates when:

$$T_{op} \geq T_{resist} + T_{friction}$$

For equilibrium of the disc:

$$T_{op} = T_{spring} + T_{friction}$$

This produces a steady-state angular displacement of the disc, and when displacement exceeds a set value, the trip contacts close.

### 2.1.6 Types of Induction Disc Relays (KTU Categorisation)

| Type | Function | Application |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Wattmetric (or Power) Type** | $T \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | Directional overcurrent / directional power |
| **Non-Wattmetric Induction Relay** | $T \propto I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | Pure overcurrent (current-current induction) |
| **Induction Cup Relay** | Very fast (1–2 cycles), high torque/inertia | Bus-bar, generator protection |
| **Induction Disc Relay** | Slower (5–30 cycles), used for IDMT | Overcurrent, earth-fault relays |

### 2.1.7 Pickup Adjustment in Induction Disc Relay

The pickup current is varied by changing the number of turns on the operating coil that are effectively in circuit. This is done by a **plug bridge** on the top of the relay — the **Plug Setting (PS)** is labelled as a percentage of the CT secondary rating (typically 50%, 75%, 100%, 125%, 150%, 175%, 200%).

## 2.2 Static Relays — Block-Level Architecture

### 2.2.1 Definition (KTU)

A **static relay** uses semiconductor devices (transistors, diodes, op-amps, comparators) and **no moving parts** to perform the measurement, comparison, and decision functions. Inputs are analogue quantities from CTs/PTs; outputs are electronic signals to the trip coil.

### 2.2.2 General Block Diagram of a Static Relay

```
                 ┌──────────────────┐
   From CT/PT ──▶│  Input CT/PT     │
                 │  (Isolation)     │
                 └────────┬─────────┘
                          │ (scaled ac signal)
                          ▼
                 ┌──────────────────┐
                 │  Rectifier        │
                 │  (AC → DC)        │
                 └────────┬─────────┘
                          │ (DC proportional to input)
                          ▼
                 ┌──────────────────┐
                 │  Level Detector  │
                 │  (Comparator)    │  ← Reference voltage = Pickup setting
                 └────────┬─────────┘
                          │ (Logic High / Low)
                          ▼
                 ┌──────────────────┐
                 │  Time-Delay      │
                 │  Circuit         │  ← IDMT or definite-time
                 └────────┬─────────┘
                          │
                          ▼
                 ┌──────────────────┐
                 │  Output Stage    │  ← Triggers trip SCR/coil
                 │  (SCR / Trans.)  │
                 └──────────────────┘
```

### 2.2.3 Block Diagram — Static Overcurrent Relay

A static overcurrent relay has a **current-sensing CT** feeding a **rectifier**, then a **DC level detector** (op-amp comparator) whose reference is the pickup setting. A **timing circuit** (R-C integrator with op-amp) gives IDMT or definite-time delay. The output drives an **SCR** that pulses the trip coil.

### 2.2.4 Block Diagram — Static Instantaneous Overcurrent Relay

It is identical to the above, **except that the timing circuit is bypassed** (or set to near-zero delay). The instant the comparator flips, the trip is issued — typical operating time **less than 1 cycle (≈ 16.67 ms at 50 Hz)**.

## 2.3 Microprocessor-Based Relay — Architecture

### 2.3.1 Definition

A **microprocessor relay** is a relay whose logic is implemented through a programmed microprocessor (or microcontroller) instead of analogue electronic components. The hardware is still partly analogue at the input; the decision and timing are **software-based**.

### 2.3.2 Key Building Blocks (Detailed in Section 4)

- Analogue input module (signal conditioning, surge protection)
- Anti-aliasing low-pass filter
- Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit
- Analogue-to-Digital Converter (ADC)
- Microprocessor + Memory (RAM/ROM/EPROM)
- Digital I/O interface
- Display/Keyboard (HMI)
- Trip output relays

## 2.4 Numerical Relay — Definition (KTU 2024 Modern)

A **numerical relay** is a microprocessor-based relay in which the analogue signals are converted to numerical (digital) data and all decision-making is performed using **Digital Signal Processing (DSP) techniques** (e.g., Discrete Fourier Transform). It is programmable, self-testing, and communication-capable (IEC 61850).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** "Microprocessor relay" and "Numerical relay" are often used interchangeably in Indian textbooks, but technically the numerical relay performs full DSP on sampled data, while older microprocessor relays used only peak/RMS measurement. Both are listed separately in your syllabus.

## 2.5 Principles of Overcurrent, Directional, Distance, and Differential Relays

### 2.5.1 Overcurrent Relay

Operates when the current in the protected element exceeds a preset value. Two sub-types:

- **Instantaneous** — operates with no intentional time delay.
- **Definite-Time** — operates after a fixed time delay regardless of current magnitude.
- **Inverse-Time (IDMT)** — operating time decreases as current increases.

**Standard IDMT characteristic (BS 142 / IEC 60255):**

$$t_{op} = \frac{0.14 \cdot \text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1} \quad \text{seconds}$$

where PSM = (fault current) / (pickup current).

### 2.5.2 Directional Relay

Operates only when the fault current flows in a **prescribed direction** (e.g., from bus into the line). The relay senses **power direction** ($V \cdot I \cos\phi$) using a wattmetric principle.

### 2.5.3 Distance Relay

Operates on the basis of **apparent impedance** $Z = V / I$ seen from the relay location. The locus of operation is a circle (or straight line) on the **R-X plane**. Three classic types:

- **Impedance Relay** — circle centred at origin
- **Reactance Relay** — straight line parallel to R-axis
- **Mho (Admittance) Relay** — circle passing through origin

### 2.5.4 Differential Relay

Operates on the difference between currents entering and leaving the protected zone. The **operate quantity** is:

$$I_{op} = \vert \vec{I_1} - \vec{I_2} \vert$$

and the **restraint quantity** is:

$$I_{rest} = \frac{\vert \vec{I_1} + \vec{I_2} \vert}{2}$$

The relay trips if $I_{op} \geq k \cdot I_{rest}$ (where $k$ is the bias slope, typically 20%–40%).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{op} = K \cdot \phi_1 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Average operating torque of induction relay | N·m |
| 2 | $\text{PSM} = \dfrac{I_f}{I_{pickup}}$ | Plug-setting multiplier | dimensionless |
| 3 | $t_{op} = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot \text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1}$ | Standard inverse IDMT operating time | seconds |
| 4 | $t_{op} = \dfrac{K \cdot \text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^n - 1}$ | General IDMT form (n = 0.02 for standard inverse) | seconds |
| 5 | $Z_{relay} = \dfrac{V_{relay}}{I_{relay}}$ | Apparent impedance seen by distance relay | ohms |
| 6 | $Z_{reach} = m \cdot Z_{line}$ | Reach setting (m = 0.8 to 0.9 typically) | ohms |
| 7 | $I_{diff} = \vert \vec{I_1} - \vec{I_2} \vert$ | Operating current of differential relay | A |
| 8 | $I_{bias} = \dfrac{\vert \vec{I_1} + \vec{I_2} \vert}{2}$ | Restraining current of biased differential relay | A |
| 9 | $V_{res} = I \cdot Z_{set}$ | Voltage required to operate impedance relay | V |
| 10 | $I_{op} = \dfrac{V}{Z_{set}}$ | Operating current of impedance relay | A |
| 11 | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | Power sensed by directional relay | W |
| 12 | $T_{resist} = K_s \cdot \theta_m$ | Spring (controlling) torque | N·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Commit formulae 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8 to memory — these six alone cover 80% of numerical questions in relays.

### 2.6.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Overcurrent relays** protect distribution feeders, transformers, and motors.
- **Directional relays** protect parallel feeders and looped networks.
- **Distance relays** protect long EHV/HV transmission lines (since impedance correlates with fault distance).
- **Differential relays** protect transformers, generators, busbars, and motor windings — the workhorse of unit protection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Operating Torque in an Induction Disc Relay

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let two coils produce two alternating fluxes:

$$\phi_1(t) = \Phi_1 \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

$$\phi_2(t) = \Phi_2 \cdot \sin(\omega t + \alpha)$$

The two coils are mounted on poles that are **displaced in space by angle $\theta$** (most commonly $\theta = 90°$ for maximum torque).

### 3.1.2 Induced EMFs and Eddy Currents

The flux $\phi_1$ induces an EMF $e_1$ in the disc, lagging $\phi_1$ by $90°$:

$$e_1 \propto \frac{d\phi_1}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e_1 \propto \Phi_1 \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

The induced current in the disc (assuming a highly resistive path, so current is in phase with EMF):

$$i_1 \propto \Phi_1 \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

Similarly, flux $\phi_2$ induces a current $i_2$ in the disc:

$$i_2 \propto \Phi_2 \cdot \cos(\omega t + \alpha)$$

### 3.1.3 Force and Torque

The instantaneous force on the conductor is $F \propto B \cdot I \cdot l$, hence instantaneous torque on the disc is:

$$T(t) = k_1 \cdot \phi_2(t) \cdot i_1(t) \cdot \cos\theta - k_1 \cdot \phi_1(t) \cdot i_2(t) \cdot \cos\theta$$

(There are two interaction terms; one is driving, the other is opposing, depending on the spatial angle between the flux and the eddy-current path.)

Substituting and simplifying for $\theta = 90°$ (so $\cos\theta = 0$ and we use the cross-product form):

$$T(t) = K \cdot \phi_1(t) \cdot \phi_2(t) \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot (\text{spatial factor})$$

### 3.1.4 Time-Averaged Torque

We now compute the average of $T(t)$ over one complete cycle:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T T(t) \, dt$$

The key integral is:

$$\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\omega t + \alpha) \, dt = \frac{1}{2}\cos\alpha$$

But the dominant (operational) term involves the **orthogonal** cross-product (because the two fluxes are $90°$ apart in space):

$$T_{avg} = K \cdot \Phi_1 \cdot \Phi_2 \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \sin\alpha$$

### 3.1.5 Final Operating Torque Equation

$$\boxed{T_{op} = K \cdot \Phi_1 \cdot \Phi_2 \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \sin\alpha}$$

**Conclusion of the derivation:**

- $T_{op} \propto \Phi_1 \cdot \Phi_2$, so it is a product-type relay (suitable for power, directional).
- $\theta = 90°$ gives maximum torque — the design choice.
- $\alpha$ must NOT be $0°$ or $180°$ (otherwise $\sin\alpha = 0$ and no torque).
- For an overcurrent relay: $\phi_1 \propto I_1$, $\phi_2 \propto I_2$, hence $T_{op} \propto I_1 \cdot I_2$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Operating Time of an IDMT Relay

### 3.2.1 The Induction Disc Equation of Motion

For the aluminium disc, the equation of motion is:

$$J \frac{d^2\theta_m}{dt^2} + D \frac{d\theta_m}{dt} + K_s \cdot \theta_m = T_{op} - T_{f}$$

where:
- $J$ = moment of inertia of disc
- $D$ = damping coefficient
- $K_s$ = spring constant
- $T_f$ = friction torque

### 3.2.2 Steady-State (Slow Speed) Approximation

At operating speeds of the disc, the inertia term is negligible. Also, the friction term is small. So we get:

$$D \frac{d\theta_m}{dt} \approx T_{op} \propto I^2 \quad \text{(for overcurrent relay)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{d\theta_m}{dt} = K' \cdot I^2$$

The operating time $t_{op}$ is the time for the disc to rotate through angle $\theta_{op}$ (the contact-closing angle):

$$t_{op} = \int_0^{\theta_{op}} \frac{d\theta_m}{K' \cdot I^2}$$

For constant fault current $I_f$:

$$t_{op} = \frac{\theta_{op}}{K' \cdot I_f^2}$$

### 3.2.3 Normalisation to PSM

Recall $\text{PSM} = I_f / I_{pickup}$. Hence $I_f = \text{PSM} \cdot I_{pickup}$, and:

$$I_f^2 = \text{PSM}^2 \cdot I_{pickup}^2$$

Substituting:

$$t_{op} = \frac{\theta_{op}}{K' \cdot I_{pickup}^2 \cdot \text{PSM}^2} = \frac{A}{\text{PSM}^2}$$

where $A$ is a constant that includes the mechanical design and pickup current. The TMS is a setting factor that scales this:

$$t_{op} = \frac{0.14 \cdot \text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1}$$

(The exact constants and exponent 0.02 come from the BS 142 standard which fits a curve to empirical relay tests — the 0.02 exponent gives the "Standard Inverse" shape.)

### 3.2.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** A 5 A, 3 s IDMT overcurrent relay with TMS = 1.0, PS = 100% (i.e., pickup = 5 A). Find operating time for a fault current of 20 A.

**Step 1:** Compute PSM.

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{I_f}{I_{pickup}} = \frac{20}{5} = 4.0$$

**Step 2:** Apply IDMT equation.

$$t_{op} = \frac{0.14 \times 1.0}{4.0^{0.02} - 1} = \frac{0.14}{1.0578 - 1} = \frac{0.14}{0.0578} = 2.42 \text{ s}$$

**Answer:** $t_{op} \approx 2.42$ seconds.

## 3.3 Derivation: Operating Principle of an Impedance (Distance) Relay

### 3.3.1 The Concept

The apparent impedance seen by a relay located at one end of a transmission line is:

$$Z_{apparent} = \frac{V_{relay}}{I_{relay}}$$

For a fault at distance $\ell$ along the line of impedance $z$ per unit length (ohm/km):

$$Z_{apparent} = z \cdot \ell$$

### 3.3.2 Operating Boundary on R-X Plane

A distance relay operates when:

$$\vert Z_{apparent} \vert \leq \vert Z_{set} \vert$$

Equivalently, the relay operates inside a **circle of radius $Z_{set}$** centred at the origin of the R-X plane. This is the **impedance relay characteristic**.

### 3.3.3 Reactance Relay Characteristic

A pure reactance relay operates when:

$$X_{apparent} \leq X_{set}$$

i.e., a horizontal line at $X = X_{set}$ on the R-X plane.

### 3.3.4 Mho (Admittance) Relay Characteristic

The mho relay characteristic is a circle **passing through the origin**, given by:

$$(R - R_c)^2 + (X - X_c)^2 = r^2$$

where the circle is tangent to the R-axis at the origin. The centre is at $(R_c, X_c) = (Z_{set}/2, 0)$ and the radius is $Z_{set}/2$. The relay operates when the apparent impedance point falls **inside** this circle.

### 3.3.5 Mathematical Condition for Mho Relay Operation

The relay operates when the **admittance component perpendicular to the set impedance** exceeds zero. Using complex phasors:

$$\Re\left( \frac{1}{Z_{set}^*}\left(Z_{set} - Z_{apparent}\right) \right) \geq 0$$

This simplifies (after manipulation) to:

$$\vert Z_{apparent} - \frac{Z_{set}}{2} \vert \leq \frac{\vert Z_{set} \vert}{2}$$

which is exactly the circle equation stated above.

## 3.4 Derivation: Biased Differential Relay Characteristic

### 3.4.1 Operating and Restraining Currents

For a biased (or percentage) differential relay with two CT secondaries $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$I_{op} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$$

$$I_{rest} = \frac{\vert I_1 + I_2 \vert}{2}$$

### 3.4.2 Operating Criterion

The relay operates when:

$$I_{op} \geq k \cdot I_{rest}$$

where $k$ is the **bias slope** (typically 0.2 to 0.4, i.e., 20% to 40%).

### 3.4.3 Characteristic Curve in the Operating Plane

Plotting $I_{op}$ (y-axis) versus $I_{rest}$ (x-axis) gives a straight line through the origin with slope $k$. The relay operates for points **above** this line. This is the **bias characteristic** — it provides stability against external faults (where $I_1 \approx I_2$ due to CT saturation effects) while still allowing sensitive operation for internal faults.

### 3.4.4 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style)

**Given:** A biased differential relay with slope $k = 0.3$. For an external fault, CT primary currents are $I_1 = 1000 \text{ A}$ and $I_2 = 990 \text{ A}$ (CT saturation). Determine whether the relay maloperates.

**Step 1:** Compute operating current.

$$I_{op} = \vert 1000 - 990 \vert = 10 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Compute restraining current.

$$I_{rest} = \frac{1000 + 990}{2} = 995 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Compute threshold.

$$k \cdot I_{rest} = 0.3 \times 995 = 298.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4:** Compare.

$$I_{op} = 10 \text{ A} \quad \ll \quad 298.5 \text{ A}$$

**Conclusion:** The relay does NOT operate. The bias characteristic prevents false tripping during CT saturation for external faults. ✅

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Static Overcurrent Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Line CT Input] --> B[Intermediate CT or Auxiliary CT]
    B --> C[Bridge Rectifier AC to DC]
    C --> D[DC Level Detector Comparator]
    D --> E[Time Delay Circuit R-C Integrator with Op-amp]
    E --> F[SCR Output Stage]
    F --> G[Trip Coil of Circuit Breaker]
    REF[DC Reference = Pickup Setting] --> D
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Static Instantaneous Overcurrent Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Line CT Input] --> B[Rectifier]
    B --> C[Comparator with Pickup Reference]
    C --> D[SCR Output Stage]
    D --> E[Trip Coil]
    REF[Pickup Reference Voltage] --> C
    note1[No Timing Circuit - Trip in less than 1 cycle] -.-> C
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Microprocessor-Based Overcurrent Relay

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Primary CTs and PTs] --> B[Signal Conditioning - Surge Protection]
    B --> C[Anti-Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    C --> D[Sampler and Hold S/H]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    E --> F[Microprocessor CPU with RAM ROM EPROM]
    F --> G[Output Relays for Trip Command]
    F --> H[Display and Keyboard HMI]
    F --> I[Communication Port RS-485 / SCADA]
    F --> J[Watchdog Timer Self-Test]
```

## 4.4 Flow Chart — Microprocessor Overcurrent Relay Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start]) --> I[Initialise Hardware and Read Settings]
    I --> R[Read Analog Samples of Current and Voltage]
    R --> F[Apply Digital Filter - Anti-Aliasing]
    F --> CALC[Compute RMS Current and Impedance]
    CALC --> CHK{Is Current above Pickup Value}
    CHK -->|No| R
    CHK -->|Yes| PSM[Compute PSM = I_fault / I_pickup]
    PSM --> IDMT[Compute t_op from IDMT Equation]
    IDMT --> WAIT[Start Countdown Timer]
    WAIT --> T{Time Elapsed equals t_op}
    T -->|No| WAIT
    T -->|Yes| TRIP[Issue Trip Pulse to Circuit Breaker]
    TRIP --> LOG[Record Fault Event in Memory]
    LOG --> R
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Numerical Relay (Modern DSP Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CT and PT Inputs] --> B[Isolation Transformer and Surge Arrestor]
    B --> C[Programmable Gain Amplifier]
    C --> D[Low Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    D --> E[Sample and Hold Multiplexer]
    E --> F[High Resolution ADC 16 bit]
    F --> G[DSP Processor]
    G --> H[DFT / FFT Algorithm]
    H --> I[Protection Algorithm Library]
    I --> J[Trip Logic Matrix]
    J --> K[Output Relays to Breakers]
    G --> L[LCD / HMI Display]
    G --> M[IEC 61850 Communication Port]
    G --> N[Event Recorder and Fault Locator]
    G --> O[Self-Test and Watchdog]
```

## 4.6 R-X Diagram — Distance Relay Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[R-X Plane for Distance Relay Characteristics] --> B[Impedance Relay - Circle centred at origin radius Z_set]
    A --> C[Reactance Relay - Horizontal line at X = X_set]
    A --> D[Mho Relay - Circle passing through origin diameter Z_set]
    A --> E[Quadrilateral Relay - Polygon with independent X and R reach]
```

## 4.7 Comparative Technology Map — Relay Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EM[Electromechanical Relay 1930s to 1970s] --> ST[Static Analogue Relay 1960s to 1980s]
    ST --> MP[Microprocessor Relay 1980s to 2000s]
    MP --> NUM[Numerical Digital Relay 2000s onwards]
    NUM --> DIG[Digital Substation IEC 61850]
```

## 4.8 Directional Relay — Wattmetric Working

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Voltage from PT] --> MUL[Multiplier / Hall Effect Sensor]
    I[Current from CT] --> MUL
    MUL --> INT[Integrator to compute VI cos phi]
    INT --> CMP[Comparator with Zero Reference]
    CMP --> OUT[Output - Operates if P is positive and exceeds setting]
    REF[Threshold Power] --> CMP
```
